proposals.title: Fine-mapping complex traits by integrating additive, dominance, and pairwise epistatic variance in a unified Bayesian framework
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and fine-mapping predominantly model additive genetic effects, yet a significant portion of trait heritability, particularly for complex traits, may be hidden in non-additive and epistatic interactions. This 'missing heritability' problem persists despite large sample sizes, suggesting current models are incomplete. Incorporating these interactions into fine-mapping is crucial for identifying true causal variants and understanding the full genetic architecture of traits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling dominance and pairwise epistatic variance components within a fine-mapping framework will improve the resolution of causal variant identification and provide more accurate estimates of the proportion of phenotypic variance explained by specific genomic regions.
proposals.method: We will extend Bayesian sparse linear mixed models (BSLMM) and fine-mapping methods like FINEMAP to include variance components for dominance and pairwise epistasis. The approach will use a computationally efficient variance component estimation method, building on the GREML framework from GCTA, to partition phenotypic variance. We will employ a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling scheme to jointly infer the posterior probability of causality for individual SNPs and SNP-SNP interaction pairs, leveraging summary statistics from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) and the FarmGTEx resource for functional annotation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available fine-mapping tool that accounts for non-additive effects, moving beyond purely additive models. It will deliver improved fine-mapped variant sets for complex traits and quantify the contribution of epistasis to local heritability, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' challenge in specific loci.
proposals.novelty: While variance component models estimate genome-wide non-additive variance, integrating these components directly into a probabilistic fine-mapping model to pinpoint specific interacting causal variants is a novel synthesis. This moves from partitioning aggregate variance to identifying specific variant-variant interactions with causal posterior probabilities.

proposals.title: Resolving the Missing Heritability of Complex Traits by Integrating Structural Variants and Rare Alleles into a Genomic Whole-Genome Feature Model
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of GWAS and SNP-based genomic prediction, a significant fraction of trait heritability remains unexplained ('missing heritability'), particularly for traits like height. Pre-2026 evidence suggests that rare alleles and structural variants (SVs), poorly tagged by common SNP arrays, may harbor substantial causal variation. The Genomic Whole-genome Feature Model (GWFM) provides a framework to partition variance across genomic annotations but has been primarily applied to common SNPs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the contributions of structural variant classes (e.g., CNVs, inversions) and rare single-nucleotide variants (MAF < 0.01) within the GWFM framework will significantly increase the proportion of additive genetic variance explained for complex traits and improve the biological interpretability of heritability estimates.
proposals.method: We will extend the GWFM to incorporate SV calls from whole-genome sequencing (WGS) data (e.g., from the FarmGTEx project or UK Biobank) and rare alleles, creating new annotation categories. The model will use a Bayesian mixed-model approach with a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to estimate variance components attributable to each feature class (common SNPs, rare SNPs, deletions, duplications). We will validate the model by comparing its variance component estimates and predictive accuracy for height and other complex traits against standard GBLUP and SNP-based GWFM.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative estimate of the heritability contribution from SVs and rare alleles, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' problem. It will deliver a refined, publicly available GWFM framework that leverages WGS data to improve genomic prediction and enhance our understanding of the genetic architecture of complex traits.
proposals.novelty: While SVs and rare variants are known to be important, their systematic integration into a variance partitioning model like GWFM to quantify their aggregate contribution to heritability, beyond single-variant association tests, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of quantitative genetics and WGS analytics.

proposals.title: Quantifying Non-Additive Genetic Variance and Epistatic Interactions for Body Mass Index in Large-Scale Biobanks
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of additive models in genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for traits like BMI, a significant portion of the estimated SNP-based heritability remains unexplained, suggesting missing genetic variance. This 'missing heritability' could be partly attributed to non-additive effects, including dominance and epistasis, which are typically ignored in standard analyses. Investigating these effects is crucial for a complete understanding of genetic architecture and improving the accuracy of genetic prediction models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a substantial proportion of the genetic variance for BMI is non-additive, arising from dominance effects and pairwise epistatic interactions between common genetic variants. We further hypothesize that explicitly modeling these interactions will improve the phenotypic variance explained and the predictive accuracy of polygenic scores.
proposals.method: We will apply variance component models using restricted maximum likelihood (REML) in large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank) to partition the phenotypic variance of BMI into additive, dominance, and epistatic components. The epistatic variance will be modeled using a Hadamard product of genomic relationship matrices (as per the extended GREML framework). We will also employ a two-step approach: first, identifying loci with significant marginal additive effects, and then testing for pairwise interactions between these top loci using regression-based tests, adjusting for population structure and relatedness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, direct estimate of the proportion of phenotypic variance for BMI attributable to non-additive genetic effects in human populations. It will generate a map of significant epistatic interactions, offering biological insights into gene networks influencing obesity. The findings will challenge the standard additive assumption and inform the development of next-generation genetic prediction models.
proposals.novelty: While non-additive variance has been studied in plant and animal breeding, robust estimation in human complex traits has been limited by sample size and computational methods. This proposal leverages unprecedented biobank-scale data and advanced variance partitioning techniques, moving beyond theoretical discussions to provide empirical, genome-wide estimates of epistasis for a major human trait.

proposals.title: Quantifying Sex-Specific Genetic Effects and Gene-by-Sex Interactions for BMI in Large-Scale Biobanks
proposals.motivation: Despite high heritability estimates for BMI, a significant portion of its genetic architecture remains unexplained. Observational differences in BMI distribution and related disease risk between sexes suggest potential sex-specific genetic effects or gene-by-sex interactions (GxS), which could contribute to this 'missing heritability'. Systematically testing the uniformity of genetic effects across sexes for established BMI loci is a critical, unresolved question in complex trait genetics.
proposals.hypothesis: The genetic architecture of BMI is not uniform across sexes, with a subset of common and rare variants exhibiting significant sex-differentiated effects or gene-by-sex interactions.
proposals.method: We will apply sex-stratified and interaction genome-wide association studies (GWAS) in large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank), using linear mixed models to estimate additive genetic variance separately in males and females. For identified loci, we will fit a GxS interaction term in a joint model. We will extend this to rare variants using gene-based burden tests. Significance will be assessed via likelihood ratio tests comparing models with and without sex-specific or interaction terms, controlling for population structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, large-scale map of sex-differentiated genetic effects for BMI, directly quantifying the contribution of GxS to its heritability. It will identify specific loci with heterogeneous effects, offering new biological insights into sexual dimorphism in adiposity and informing more precise, sex-aware polygenic risk scores.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have noted mean sex differences, a comprehensive, variance-component-based dissection of sex-specific additive and interaction effects for BMI at both common and rare variant levels, leveraging biobank-scale data, has not been conclusively performed, moving beyond simple stratification to formal interaction testing.

proposals.title: Estimating the Contribution of Epistatic and Gene-Environment Interactions to the Heritability and Variance of Body Mass Index
proposals.motivation: Despite genome-wide association studies identifying hundreds of loci for BMI, a significant portion of its heritability remains unaccounted for, a phenomenon known as 'missing heritability.' Pre-2026 research, including Goddard's own work, suggests that additive genetic variance estimates may be inflated by factors like gametic phase disequilibrium, while non-additive (epistatic) and gene-environment (GxE) interactions are hypothesized to contribute to trait variance but are poorly quantified in human populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a substantial proportion of the variance and 'missing heritability' of BMI can be explained by genome-wide epistatic (GxG) interactions and by interactions between common genetic variants and specific, measurable environmental factors.
proposals.method: We will apply and extend variance component models, building on Goddard's expertise in additive and rare variant analyses. Using large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank), we will estimate the variance explained by pairwise epistatic interactions across the genome using a method like GREML-HE or a similar linear mixed model framework that incorporates an interaction relationship matrix. Concurrently, we will model GxE interactions for defined environmental variables (e.g., socioeconomic status, physical activity) by partitioning genetic effects into environment-specific components and testing for heterogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first comprehensive, quantitative estimates of the contribution of genome-wide epistasis to BMI heritability in humans. It will also map specific GxE interactions, moving beyond aggregate estimates to actionable biological and environmental insights. The findings will directly address the sources of 'missing heritability' and refine our understanding of genetic architecture.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 studies focus on additive effects or specific variant interactions, this proposal systematically quantifies the aggregate contribution of genome-wide epistasis to BMI variance, a key unresolved question. It uniquely integrates this with a parallel, rigorous search for GxE effects within the same statistical framework used for heritability estimation.

proposals.title: Resolving the Missing Heritability of BMI: A Genome-Wide Search for Rare, Penetrant Variants Using Whole-Genome Sequencing and Variance-Component Models
proposals.motivation: Despite large-scale GWAS identifying hundreds of common variants for BMI, a significant portion of its heritability remains unexplained ('missing'). Rare variants with larger effect sizes are a prime candidate for this missing heritability but have been difficult to detect due to limited power in array-based studies. This project directly addresses this gap by leveraging whole-genome sequencing (WGS) to systematically search for rare, penetrant variants within both known and novel loci.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare (minor allele frequency < 1%), penetrant coding and regulatory variants, aggregated within genes and genomic loci, contribute significantly to the additive genetic variance of BMI, and that these variants are not fully tagged by common GWAS SNPs.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-coverage WGS data from large, deeply phenotyped cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank). Using a variance-component framework, we will partition additive genetic variance for BMI explained by (a) common SNPs, (b) rare variants aggregated at the gene-level using burden and SKAT tests, and (c) rare variants within known BMI GWAS loci. We will extend this to a genome-wide scan for novel loci by fitting a rare-variant-specific genetic relationship matrix (GRM) constructed from rare variants, analogous to methods used for height, to estimate their total contribution and map specific regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative estimate of the proportion of BMI heritability attributable to rare variants, moving beyond speculation. It will identify specific genes and regulatory regions harboring high-impact rare variants, offering new biological insights into weight regulation. The methodological integration of variance partitioning with WGS-based rare variant mapping will serve as a blueprint for other complex traits.
proposals.novelty: While rare variant studies exist, this proposal uniquely combines a genome-wide variance decomposition approach—inspired by Goddard's work on partitioning heritability—with focused mapping in known loci, directly testing the rare variant hypothesis for missing heritability in a unified analytical framework. The application of a rare-variant-specific GRM for genome-wide scanning is a novel extension of established additive models.

proposals.title: Causal Inference on the Role of BMI in Cardiometabolic Disease: A Multi-Instrument Mendelian Randomization Study Partitioned by Genetic Architecture
proposals.motivation: Body mass index (BMI) is a major risk factor for cardiometabolic diseases, but the causal nature and magnitude of its effects on specific outcomes remain partially obscured by confounding and reverse causation. While Mendelian Randomization (MR) offers a solution, standard approaches using common variant polygenic scores may be biased by horizontal pleiotropy and fail to capture the distinct causal pathways potentially represented by rare, high-impact variants. Resolving these causal pathways is critical for targeted public health and therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that BMI-associated genetic variants, when partitioned by their frequency and effect size (common vs. rare), will reveal distinct and specific causal effects on downstream health outcomes such as type 2 diabetes, coronary artery disease, and hypertension, with rare variant instruments providing more direct estimates of the biological impact of adiposity.
proposals.method: We will construct multiple genetic instruments for BMI from large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank), partitioning variants by minor allele frequency and effect size estimates from genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and rare variant association tests. We will apply Two-Sample MR using inverse-variance weighted and robust methods (MR-Egger, weighted median) to estimate causal effects on disease outcomes from independent consortia. Sensitivity analyses will assess and correct for pleiotropy, and we will employ variance partitioning methods to quantify the contribution of each variant class to the total genetically predicted causal effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, architecture-aware causal map of BMI's role in disease, moving beyond a single aggregate estimate. It will directly test whether the 'missing heritability' of BMI, often attributed to rare variants, translates into distinct causal pathways. The findings will inform whether prevention strategies should consider the heterogeneous biological mechanisms underlying genetic predisposition to obesity.
proposals.novelty: This study integrates Goddard's expertise in variance partitioning and genetic architecture with MR, uniquely applying the method not to a single instrument but to multiple instruments defined by the underlying genetic architecture of the exposure (BMI), a novel approach pre-2026. It directly tests if different parts of the allelic spectrum instrument for biologically distinct causal pathways.

proposals.title: Leveraging Murine Co-expression QTL Networks to Prioritize Causal Genes in Human BMI GWAS Loci
proposals.motivation: Despite large GWAS for BMI, identifying the causal genes within associated loci remains a major challenge, contributing to the 'missing heritability' problem. Model organisms like mice offer rich systems genetics resources, such as expression QTL (eQTL) and co-expression QTL (co-eQTL) data, which can reveal gene regulatory networks perturbed by genetic variants. Integrating these conserved molecular networks into human genetic analysis provides a powerful, biologically informed filter to prioritize candidate genes.
proposals.hypothesis: Genes within human BMI GWAS loci that are hubs in murine adipose or hypothalamic co-expression networks, and whose connectivity is modulated by genetic variation (co-eQTLs), are more likely to be the causal genes underlying the human genetic association.
proposals.method: We will obtain co-eQTL data from large murine resources like the Hybrid Mouse Diversity Panel. For each human BMI locus from large-scale consortia (e.g., GIANT), we will map orthologous genes and test if they are central nodes in tissue-relevant co-expression networks. We will prioritize genes based on network centrality measures and the strength of their co-eQTL support. This prioritized list will be validated using human eQTL colocalization analysis and partitioned heritability estimation from rare variant burden tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, systems-genetics-informed gene prioritization method, generating a refined list of high-confidence causal genes for human BMI. It will demonstrate the utility of model organism co-regulation networks for dissecting human complex traits, moving beyond simple eQTL colocalization. The results will provide specific biological targets for functional follow-up and help explain the additive genetic variance tagged by common SNPs.
proposals.novelty: While using model organism eQTLs is established, leveraging co-expression QTLs—which capture genetic effects on gene-gene relationships—to prioritize human genes is a novel, network-based approach. It directly builds on Goddard's work on partitioning heritability by integrating cross-species, systems-level data to address gene prioritization.

proposals.title: A Meta-GWAS of Over One Million Individuals to Resolve the Missing Heritability and Genetic Architecture of Body Mass Index
proposals.motivation: Despite the high heritability of BMI, large-scale GWAS have identified variants explaining only a fraction of its genetic variance, a key example of the 'missing heritability' problem. Prior to 2026, the largest BMI GWAS included ~700,000 individuals, identifying hundreds of loci but leaving the contribution of rare variants and polygenic architecture incompletely characterized. A sample size exceeding one million is the critical next step to discover loci of smaller effect and refine genetic models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-analysis of genome-wide association studies (GWAS) exceeding 1 million individuals will identify hundreds of novel BMI-associated loci, predominantly with small effect sizes, and will allow for a more precise estimation of the contribution of rare variants and gametic phase disequilibrium to BMI heritability.
proposals.method: We will perform a fixed-effects, inverse-variance weighted meta-analysis of individual-level or summary-statistics GWAS data from international consortia (e.g., UK Biobank, GIANT, others) to achieve a total N > 1,000,000. Analysis will use established pre-2026 pipelines (e.g., METAL, SAIGE) to ensure robust association testing and population stratification control. We will apply variance component models (GREML-LDMS) to partition heritability by allele frequency and linkage disequilibrium (LD) bins, directly testing hypotheses from Goddard's work on gametic phase disequilibrium. Locus discovery and fine-mapping will be conducted using FUMA and statistical fine-mapping tools.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the most comprehensive map of genetic variants influencing BMI to date, substantially advancing the field's understanding of its polygenic architecture. It will provide the most accurate estimates to date of common and rare variant heritability, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' question. The resulting genetic resource will empower improved polygenic scores and inform biological follow-up studies.
proposals.novelty: While large meta-analyses exist, scaling to over one million individuals represents a quantitative leap expected to yield qualitatively new insights into the genetic architecture of a highly polygenic trait. The integrated analysis of discovery and variance component estimation, guided by specific theories on gametic phase disequilibrium, moves beyond simple locus cataloging to test explicit models of heritability.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Genetic Architecture of Complex Traits: A Bayesian Sparse Factor Model for Partitioning GWAS Signals into Shared and Trait-Specific Components
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of genetic variants associated with complex traits, but a substantial fraction of heritability remains unmapped. Furthermore, genetic correlations between traits are pervasive, suggesting shared genetic influences, yet methods to formally decompose GWAS summary statistics into shared and trait-specific genetic components are limited. This gap hinders our understanding of pleiotropy and the biological specificity of genetic effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Bayesian sparse factor models to multi-trait GWAS summary statistics will robustly decompose the genetic covariance structure, identifying a small number of latent genetic factors that capture shared biology and residual trait-specific genetic components that are enriched for distinct functional pathways.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian sparse latent factor model, building on frameworks like BSLMM and extensions for summary statistics. The model will take as input matrices of SNP effect sizes and their sampling covariances from multiple traits. We will employ spike-and-slab priors on factor loadings to induce sparsity and identify interpretable latent factors. Inference will be performed via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to estimate posterior distributions for the number of factors, factor loadings, and trait-specific genetic variances. We will validate the method using simulations and apply it to large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank) for anthropometric, metabolic, and disease-related traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel statistical tool to quantify and interpret the genetic overlap between complex traits, moving beyond genetic correlation estimates. It will directly address the 'missing heritability' problem by partitioning the genetic variance explained by common SNPs into structured (shared) and residual (trait-specific) components. The resulting decomposition will offer new biological insights into pleiotropic architectures and inform the design of cross-trait prediction models.
proposals.novelty: While factor models exist in genetics, their application as a formal decomposition tool for GWAS summary statistics using a fully Bayesian, sparse framework is novel. This approach explicitly models the statistical uncertainty in effect estimates and provides a principled way to separate shared genetic influences from trait-specific signals, which is non-trivial with standard multivariate methods.

proposals.title: Conditional and Joint Analysis of Rare Variant Burden Scores using Summary Statistics (RV-COJO)
proposals.motivation: A significant portion of trait heritability, particularly for complex diseases, is hypothesized to reside in rare variants, but their detection and characterization remain challenging. While the mtCOJO framework is powerful for dissecting polygenic signals from common variants using GWAS summary data, it is not designed for the aggregated burden and variance-component tests (e.g., SKAT-O) used in rare variant association studies (RVAS). This creates a critical gap in our ability to perform conditional analysis and distinguish independent rare variant signals from those mediated by common variant backgrounds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core principles of the mtCOJO framework can be adapted to summary statistics from rare variant association tests, enabling the estimation of conditionally independent rare variant effects and improving the resolution of causal gene mapping.
proposals.method: We will extend the mtCOJO model to operate on summary statistics from gene-based rare variant tests (e.g., burden test p-values and effect direction scores). The method will use external linkage disequilibrium (LD) reference panels from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) to account for correlations between rare variant burden scores and common variant genotypes. We will develop algorithms to estimate the genetic covariance between burden scores and common variants, and subsequently perform conditional analysis to derive adjusted rare variant effect estimates. Validation will be conducted using simulated exome data and real summary statistics from consortia like the UK Biobank Exome Sequencing Project.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated framework for conditional analysis of rare variant associations using summary data, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' problem. It will empower researchers to distinguish direct rare variant effects from those confounded by common polygenic signals, leading to more accurate gene discovery and functional interpretation in exome and genome sequencing studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in translating a framework designed for common single-variant statistics to the distinct data structure of gene-based rare variant tests, which requires new models for covariance estimation between aggregated burden scores and common variant LD.

proposals.title: Cross-Ancestry Conditional and Joint Analysis (mtCOJO) Reveals Ancestry-Specific and Shared Genetic Architecture for Complex Traits
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) remain overwhelmingly Eurocentric, limiting the generalizability of genetic discoveries and hindering the identification of ancestry-specific genetic effects and trans-ancestry pleiotropy. Pre-2026, multi-trait conditional and joint analysis (mtCOJO) has proven powerful for dissecting genetic correlations and causal relationships in European cohorts, but its systematic application to diverse ancestries is lacking. This gap prevents a comprehensive understanding of whether genetic architectures are consistent across populations, a key question for equitable genomic medicine and for resolving the 'missing heritability' problem in a global context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying mtCOJO to GWAS summary statistics from diverse ancestries will reveal a spectrum of genetic architectures, from highly pleiotropic, ancestry-invariant effects to ancestry-specific variant-trait associations, with significant implications for polygenic prediction and causal inference.
proposals.method: We will implement the mtCOJO framework using pre-2026 software (e.g., GCTA-COJO) and apply it to publicly available GWAS summary statistics for key complex traits (e.g., height, BMI, lipid levels) from non-European biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank non-European subsets, BioBank Japan, PAGE Consortium). We will use ancestry-matched linkage disequilibrium (LD) reference panels (e.g., from 1000 Genomes Project) for each population. The analysis will proceed in two stages: first, performing within-ancestry mtCOJO to identify conditionally independent signals; second, conducting cross-ancestry comparisons to classify variants as ancestry-specific, differentiated in effect size, or shared with consistent effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of trans-ancestry pleiotropy and ancestry-specific genetic effects for major complex traits using a conditional analysis framework. It will directly inform the portability of polygenic risk scores and highlight traits and genomic regions where genetic models differ across populations. The results will be a critical resource for prioritizing variants and loci for functional follow-up in diverse cellular and tissue contexts, such as those profiled in projects like FarmGTEx.
proposals.novelty: While mtCOJO is established, its rigorous application to compare genetic architectures across ancestries, rather than within a single (European) population, is novel. This moves beyond simple correlation analyses to identify which specific causal signals are shared or unique, addressing a core limitation of current cross-population genetic studies.

proposals.title: Defining Fundamental Non-Separability in Complex Traits: A Test of Non-Commutative Genetic Perturbations via Combinatorial Mutagenesis and Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in estimating additive and non-additive genetic variance, a formal, operational definition of fundamental non-separability—where gene effects are mechanistically inseparable—is lacking. Current models for epistasis or GxE are statistical descriptions of outcome variance, not causal tests of underlying biological interaction. This gap limits our ability to move from variance partitioning to a mechanistic understanding of how genetic variants combine to shape complex phenotypes like height.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fundamental non-separability manifests as non-commutativity in phenotypic outcomes: the effect of perturbing gene A then B differs from perturbing B then A. This provides a causal, operational criterion distinct from statistical non-additivity.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual experimental-computational pipeline. Experimentally, we will use CRISPR-based combinatorial mutagenesis in a model system (e.g., yeast or cell lines) to perform ordered genetic perturbations and measure high-dimensional phenotypic outcomes. Computationally, we will train deep neural networks (specifically, modified transformer architectures or deep interaction networks) on large-scale human population genomic data (e.g., UK Biobank) to predict phenotypic effects of variant combinations and identify in silico candidates for non-commutative interactions. The convergence of empirical perturbation data and computational predictions will test the hypothesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal causal test for fundamental non-separability, moving beyond variance estimation. It will deliver a concrete experimental-computational framework to identify specific genetic interactions where order matters, offering a direct link between statistical epistasis and mechanistic biology. This addresses the 'missing heritability' problem by clarifying the nature of non-additive effects.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in defining non-separability via non-commutativity—a concept from physics and mathematics—and creating a direct experimental test for it in genetics. This goes beyond estimating variance components or statistical epistasis coefficients to probe causal mechanism.

proposals.title: Detecting Non-Separability in the Genotype-Phenotype Map: A Framework Inspired by Quantum Decoherence
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in GWAS and variance component estimation, a substantial fraction of trait heritability remains unexplained, and the relative contributions of additive and non-additive (e.g., epistatic) genetic variance are poorly characterized. Current linear mixed models inherently assume the separability of these variance components, which may be an invalid constraint for traits governed by nonlinear biological systems, leading to biased estimates and obscured genetic architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that additive and epistatic genetic effects for complex traits are fundamentally non-separable in a manner analogous to quantum entanglement, and that this non-separability can be directly detected and quantified using statistical operators adapted from quantum information theory, without requiring a forced partition into independent variance components.
proposals.method: We will develop 'non-separability witnesses' as computational operators on genomic and phenotypic data. The core approach adapts the concept of an entanglement witness—a Hermitian operator whose expectation value signals entanglement. We will construct analogous statistical operators from high-dimensional genomic relationship matrices (GRMs) and phenotypic data, leveraging tools from random matrix theory and kernel methods. Specifically, we will test for deviations from the expectation under a purely additive, separable model by analyzing the spectral properties of transformed GRMs and the covariance structure between additive genomic projections and interaction-term residuals in large-scale datasets like the FarmGTEx project and UK Biobank.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework to diagnose when standard additive models are insufficient due to inherent non-separability, moving the field beyond variance partitioning. It will yield new metrics to characterize the joint architecture of genetic effects, offering a more nuanced explanation for missing heritability and the limitations of current polygenic prediction models for nonlinear traits.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel in its direct import of a conceptual and mathematical framework from quantum information theory to quantitative genetics, challenging the foundational separability assumption of classical models. Instead of estimating epistatic variance, it seeks to directly measure the property of non-separability itself.

proposals.title: Disentangling Evolutionary Constraint from Statistical Artifact: A Simulation-Empirical Test for the Rarity of Stabilizing Selection Signatures
proposals.motivation: Despite strong theoretical expectations, empirical evidence for widespread stabilizing selection in natural populations remains scarce. A long-standing debate persists on whether this rarity reflects a genuine evolutionary phenomenon or is an artifact of methodological limitations, such as low statistical power, fitness measure error, and environmental covariances. Resolving this is critical for understanding the maintenance of genetic variance and the constraints on adaptive evolution for complex traits.
proposals.hypothesis: The apparent rarity of stabilizing selection is not solely a methodological artifact but can be explained by a specific evolutionary mechanism: the collective action of many rare, small-effect variants, whose transient contributions to fitness surfaces are obscured by additive genetic variance estimates. This mechanism generates a distinct, multivariate signature distinguishable from noise.
proposals.method: We will develop an individual-based, forward-time simulation that integrates a realistic genetic architecture (incorporating rare variants from pre-2026 sequencing studies) with explicit models of measurement error and environmental covariance. Using this, we will generate quantitative predictions for multivariate signatures, including specific patterns in the temporal stability of fitness surface curvature and trait-fitness covariance matrices. These a priori predictions will be tested against a curated meta-dataset from long-term individual-based studies (e.g., from FarmGTEx and similar projects) using mixed models and variance partitioning to estimate additive and non-additive genetic components of fitness-trait relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, causal framework to adjudicate between competing explanations for the missing signature of stabilizing selection. It will deliver novel, testable metrics for detecting constrained evolution in empirical data and clarify the role of rare variant architecture in shaping observable fitness surfaces.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond correlative arguments by using adversarial simulation to generate falsifiable predictions that directly pit a novel genetic mechanism against established methodological critiques, a strategy not yet applied to this classic problem in evolutionary genetics.

proposals.title: Causal Discovery of Directed Epistatic Networks Underlying Complex Traits Using Observational Genomic Data
proposals.motivation: Despite significant progress in estimating additive genetic variance and mapping variants, a substantial fraction of heritability remains unexplained, with epistasis a likely contributor. Current variance partitioning methods treat epistasis as an undirected, statistical interaction, failing to reveal the causal structure or developmental sequence of genetic interactions. This limits our ability to move from statistical description to mechanistic understanding of biological pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: Causal discovery algorithms, when integrated with models for partitioning genetic variance, can infer directed networks of epistatic interactions from observational genomic and phenotypic data, distinguishing primary driver variants from conditional modifiers.
proposals.method: We will integrate linear mixed models for additive and epistatic variance estimation with constraint-based causal discovery algorithms, specifically the PC and FCI algorithms, adapted for high-dimensional genomic data. The approach will use estimated genetic values (e.g., GBLUP predictions for additive and pairwise interaction effects) as input variables. Causal structure will be learned from the conditional independence relationships among these genetic values across a large population, with directionality informed by prior knowledge of variant annotation and biological plausibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first causal, directed maps of epistatic networks for complex traits, shifting the goal from variance description to mechanistic network discovery. It will generate testable hypotheses about the hierarchy of genetic interactions and potential biological pathways, offering new targets for intervention in plant and animal breeding.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion of causal inference from epidemiology with quantitative genetics models to infer directionality in epistasis, moving beyond symmetric variance components to discover asymmetric, causal interaction networks.

proposals.title: Diagnosing the Architecture of Linkage-Failure Traits via Interpretable Nonlinear Genotype-Phenotype Maps
proposals.motivation: Classical linkage analysis famously failed for many complex traits, such as human height and psychiatric disorders, creating a persistent gap in understanding their genetic architecture. The 'missing heritability' problem, which your prior work addresses, suggests these failures may arise from architectures (e.g., extreme polygenicity, epistasis) invisible to linear additive models used in linkage and early GWAS. Systematically diagnosing the cause of these historical failures is crucial for directing future genetic research strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying flexible, nonlinear machine learning models to genotypes from pedigrees used in failed linkage studies will yield accurate phenotypic predictions and that the structure of the learned mapping function will diagnostically classify the trait's underlying genetic architecture.
proposals.method: We will apply neural networks (MLPs and CNNs) and Gaussian Processes to dense SNP data from well-powered family studies where linkage failed (e.g., for height, schizophrenia). Using pre-2026 frameworks like TensorFlow/PyTorch and GPy, we will train models to predict the trait, comparing predictive accuracy to linear additive baselines. We will then use interpretability tools (e.g., saliency maps, feature interaction scores, function space analysis of GPs) to decompose the learned mapping into signatures of polygenicity, epistasis, and dominance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, data-driven classification of traits by the architectural feature that caused linkage failure, moving beyond anecdote. It will produce a framework to recommend optimal study designs (e.g., ultra-large GWAS, family-based epistasis screens) based on the diagnosed architecture. Finally, it will demonstrate the value of interpretable machine learning as a tool for fundamental discovery in quantitative genetics.
proposals.novelty: While ML is used for prediction, its novel application here is as a diagnostic tool to explain a specific historical methodological failure, inverting the problem to use failure as a filter for architectural complexity. The explicit fusion of interpretable ML with classic linkage study data to resolve the 'missing heritability' puzzle is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Disentangling Additive Genetic Variance from Gene-Environment Correlation in Unrelated Individuals Using Genomic Structural Equation Modeling
proposals.motivation: Standard genomic heritability estimation methods, like GREML, assume no gene-environment correlation (rGE), which can lead to biased estimates. This is a critical limitation for complex traits like height, where individuals may actively shape their environments based on genetic predispositions (active rGE). Accurately partitioning variance is essential for understanding trait architecture and for accurate genomic prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling active gene-environment correlation in unrelated individuals will yield more accurate and lower estimates of narrow-sense heritability for complex traits, and will reveal the proportion of phenotypic variance attributable to rGE pathways.
proposals.method: We will extend the Genomic SEM framework to incorporate a latent 'environmental propensity' variable that is influenced by the genome. Using individual-level genotype and phenotype data from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank), we will fit models where genetic effects are partitioned into a direct path to the trait and an indirect path mediated by the environmental propensity. This will be implemented using restricted maximum likelihood (REML) in a multi-trait model, with the genetic relationship matrix (GRM) constructed from common and rare variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method to estimate and correct for rGE bias in heritability studies of unrelated individuals. It will deliver revised, likely lower, heritability estimates for key traits and quantify the contribution of active rGE, advancing the 'missing heritability' mapping agenda by clarifying the target parameter.
proposals.novelty: While rGE is acknowledged, most pre-2026 methods for unrelated samples either ignore it or require family designs. This proposal develops a novel, tractable model to directly estimate rGE parameters using genomic data from unrelated individuals, bridging quantitative genetics and causal inference.

proposals.title: Mapping Dominance Variance Contributions of Rare and Common Variants to Complex Traits Using Whole-Genome Regression
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and genomic heritability analyses have largely focused on additive genetic effects, often reporting negligible contributions from dominance variance for complex traits like human height. However, this conclusion may stem from methodological limitations in modeling non-additive effects across the full allele frequency spectrum, particularly for rare variants. Resolving this is crucial for a complete understanding of genetic architecture and for improving the accuracy of genetic prediction models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dominance variance is non-negligible for specific classes of genetic variants, particularly rare variants and variants within functionally constrained genomic regions, and that its contribution has been systematically underestimated in previous whole-genome analyses.
proposals.method: We will apply a whole-genome regression framework, extending the GREML-LDMS approach used in the 'missing heritability' papers, to partition phenotypic variance into additive and dominance components stratified by variant minor allele frequency (MAF) and functional annotation. Using large-scale genomic datasets (e.g., UK Biobank), we will fit a model including both additive and dominance relationship matrices constructed from subsets of variants. The analysis will be implemented using optimized mixed-model solvers (e.g., MTG2 or GCTA) to handle the computational burden of dual variance component estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of dominance variance contributions across the allele frequency and functional spectrum for major complex traits. It will clarify whether the apparent absence of dominance is a general feature or an artifact of aggregation, directly testing a fundamental assumption in quantitative genetics.
proposals.novelty: While additive variance partitioning by MAF is established, a parallel, systematic investigation of dominance variance using the same stratified framework is novel. It moves beyond testing genome-wide dominance to identify specific genomic contexts where non-additive effects are most likely to be detectable.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Bias: A Formal Model of Linkage Disequilibrium Effects on Variance Component and Heritability Estimates
proposals.motivation: Genome-based restricted maximum likelihood (GREML) methods are foundational for estimating additive genetic variance and heritability from SNP data. However, it is widely acknowledged that linkage disequilibrium (LD) between causal variants can bias these estimates, but the magnitude, direction, and genomic architecture-dependence of this bias remain poorly quantified. This gap limits the interpretability of heritability estimates from genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and the FarmGTEx project, especially for traits influenced by rare variants in high-LD regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LD between causal variants introduces a systematic, quantifiable bias in GREML-based variance component estimates, which is a function of the local LD structure, the minor allele frequency spectrum of causal variants, and the genetic architecture of the trait.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework to model the expected bias in GREML estimates due to LD between causal variants, extending concepts from the 'gametic phase disequilibrium' paper. This will be implemented in simulation studies using real genomic data (e.g., from FarmGTEx or UK Biobank) to generate phenotypes under controlled causal architectures. We will specifically test the performance of existing GREML implementations (e.g., GCTA) and proposed corrections using LD-pruned or LD-adjusted relationship matrices against our derived expectations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, interpretable model for a major known source of bias in heritability estimation, moving beyond qualitative acknowledgment to quantitative prediction. It will deliver practical guidance on when and how LD biases are most severe, directly informing the interpretation of 'missing heritability' maps and the design of future GREML analyses in human and agricultural genetics.
proposals.novelty: While the biasing effect of LD is known, a formal, generalizable model quantifying its impact on variance components as a function of causal architecture is lacking. This proposal integrates Goddard's prior work on gametic phase disequilibrium and missing heritability mapping to directly address this gap with a causal, rather than purely statistical, perspective.

proposals.title: Mapping the Genetic Architecture of High-Epistasis Complex Traits: A Focus on Variance Decomposition and Rare Variant Interactions
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of additive models in genome-wide association studies (GWAS), a significant portion of the 'missing heritability' for many complex traits remains unexplained. Pre-2026 research, including work by Goddard and colleagues, suggests non-additive genetic variance, particularly epistasis, may be a substantial contributor, but its genome-wide detection and quantification have been methodologically challenging and trait-specific. Identifying traits where epistatic variance is highest provides a crucial entry point to understand the conditions and genetic architectures that favor non-additive effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific complex traits, identifiable from prior variance component analyses, harbor significantly elevated levels of genome-wide epistatic variance, and that this epistasis is partially attributable to interactions involving rare genetic variants poorly captured by standard additive models.
proposals.method: We will first perform a meta-analysis of published GREML-based variance component studies (pre-2026) to curate a list of traits with the highest point estimates for epistatic variance (Vg×g). For top candidate traits (e.g., from human biobanks or FarmGTEx resources), we will apply and extend methods for genome-wide epistasis detection. This will involve using an updated GREML framework incorporating a realized epistatic relationship matrix, as informed by Goddard's work on gametic phase disequilibrium, and specifically partitioning variance from rare variant interactions using a stratified approach based on minor allele frequency.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a ranked catalog of traits where epistasis is a major genetic component, moving beyond point estimates to provide mapped loci and variant pairs. It will directly address the 'missing heritability' problem by quantifying a often-neglected variance component and test the role of rare variants in epistasis. The results will guide future studies on trait biology and inform genomic prediction models in agriculture and medicine.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies estimated global epistatic variance, this proposal systematically identifies *which traits* are most affected and actively searches for the interacting variants, with a specific focus on integrating rare variants into the epistasis model, a key gap in pre-2026 methodologies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Pedigree Structure on the Estimation of Additive Genetic Variance and Heritability from Genome-Wide Variants
proposals.motivation: Heritability estimates for complex traits, derived from genome-wide SNP data in unrelated individuals, often rely on assumptions about the distribution of causal variants and linkage disequilibrium (LD). These estimates can be confounded by factors like population stratification and cryptic relatedness, and may not capture all additive genetic variance, contributing to the 'missing heritability' problem. Using simulated data with known pedigree structures provides a ground-truth framework to validate and calibrate these estimation methods, a critical step before application to real-world datasets like FarmGTEx.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that heritability estimates from genome-wide variants in samples of unrelated individuals will systematically differ from the true additive genetic variance when validated against simulated pedigree data, and that incorporating known family structures will improve the accuracy and precision of these estimates.
proposals.method: We will use forward-time genetic simulation software (e.g., SLiM) to generate large-scale, realistic genomic data for complex traits across defined pedigree structures (e.g., multi-generational families, half-sibs). True additive genetic values will be simulated from a mixture of common and rare causal variants. We will then apply standard GREML-based methods (as implemented in GCTA) and LD Score Regression to the simulated 'unrelated' subsets and compare these estimates to those obtained using pedigree-based methods (e.g., AIREML) on the full family data, and to the known simulated truth.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative assessment of the biases and precision of SNP-based heritability estimation methods under controlled, known genetic architectures. It will yield practical guidelines for interpreting estimates from biobank-scale studies of unrelateds and demonstrate the value of integrating family data to anchor genomic predictions. The results will directly inform the analysis of missing heritability in human and farm animal phenotypes.
proposals.novelty: While simulation studies exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on systematically dissecting the discrepancy between pedigree-derived and SNP-derived variance estimates using modern, whole-genome simulation frameworks that explicitly model family structures and a spectrum of causal variants, moving beyond simple population genetic models.

proposals.title: Chromosome-Scale Partitioning of Additive and Non-Additive Genetic Variance in Complex Traits
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide heritability estimates often fail to account for the full genetic architecture of complex traits, a gap known as 'missing heritability.' While non-additive effects (e.g., dominance, epistasis) are hypothesized to contribute, their genomic localization and quantitative impact remain poorly characterized. Partitioning genetic variance at a chromosome scale, rather than genome-wide, is a critical step to identify specific genomic regions harboring significant non-additive variation, moving beyond aggregate estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying variance component models chromosome-by-chromosome will reveal significant, localized contributions of non-additive genetic effects (specifically dominance variance) to complex traits, which are obscured in whole-genome analyses. These contributions will vary across chromosomes, reflecting distinct architectural patterns.
proposals.method: We will apply a linear mixed model (LMM) framework, as used in GREML and related methods, to large-scale genomic datasets (e.g., UK Biobank). For each autosome, we will construct separate genomic relationship matrices (GRMs) for additive and dominance effects using imputed SNP data. Using restricted maximum likelihood (REML), we will estimate the proportion of phenotypic variance explained by additive and dominance components per chromosome. We will validate the approach using simulated traits with known architecture and apply it to human height, a well-studied polygenic trait.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed map of per-chromosome additive and non-additive variance for major complex traits, directly quantifying the localization of missing heritability. It will establish a methodological framework for fine-scale variance partitioning, informing future efforts to identify specific loci involved in non-additive interactions and refining our understanding of genetic architecture.
proposals.novelty: While whole-genome partitioning of additive and non-additive variance exists, a systematic chromosome-scale application to localize these effects is novel. This approach moves beyond asking *if* non-additive variance exists to pinpointing *where* in the genome it is most consequential, leveraging established LMM methods in a new, spatially resolved context.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Distribution of SNP Effect Sizes for Complex Traits: A Direct Test of the Infinitesimal Model
proposals.motivation: The 'missing heritability' problem persists, where SNP-based heritability estimates from GWAS often fall short of pedigree-based estimates. The classical infinitesimal model, which assumes a uniform distribution of tiny effects from many loci, is a foundational but largely untested assumption for complex traits. Resolving the true distribution of effect sizes is crucial for understanding genetic architecture and improving genomic prediction, especially for traits like height where rare variants may contribute.
proposals.hypothesis: The heritability of complex traits is explained by a finite number of causal variants whose effect sizes follow a non-uniform, heavy-tailed distribution, thereby violating the equal-effects infinitesimal model.
proposals.method: We will apply and extend variance component models, such as BOLT-REML and LDAK, to large-scale genomic data (e.g., UK Biobank, FarmGTEx resources) to estimate the contribution of SNPs binned by minor allele frequency (MAF) and linkage disequilibrium (LD) score. We will explicitly model the relationship between SNP heritability and MAF/LD using a scaling parameter (α) to infer the underlying effect size distribution. Bayesian sparse linear mixed models (e.g., BayesR) will be used to directly estimate the proportion of variants in discrete effect size categories, testing for a heavy-tailed distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative test of the infinitesimal model for key human and agricultural traits. It will clarify the relative contributions of common and rare variants to heritability and genetic variance, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' debate. The findings will inform the development of more accurate genomic prediction models by specifying the optimal weighting of variants.
proposals.novelty: While variance partitioning by MAF is established, this proposal integrates multiple complementary statistical approaches (scaling laws and sparse Bayesian models) on the same datasets to robustly characterize the full effect size distribution, moving beyond aggregate variance estimates to challenge the infinitesimal assumption directly.

proposals.title: A Unified Genomic Control Framework for Stratification-Robust Heritability Estimation in Complex Traits
proposals.motivation: Population stratification remains a pervasive confounder in genomic analyses, inflating heritability estimates from genome-wide data by inducing spurious genetic correlations. Current genomic control methods, while foundational, are often simplistic, assuming uniform inflation across the genome, which fails to account for the complex, trait-specific patterns of stratification seen in diverse cohorts. This limitation directly impedes the accurate estimation and mapping of 'missing heritability' for complex traits like height, a central theme in Dr. Goddard's recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a method modeling stratification as a structured, trait-specific covariance component, rather than a uniform genomic inflation factor, will yield more accurate and robust heritability estimates across diverse population structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel mixed-model framework extending GREML-LDMS and BOLT-REML approaches. The method will explicitly partition the genetic relatedness matrix (GRM) into components reflecting fine-scale population structure (e.g., using principal components of common variants) and a residual polygenic component. We will implement a REML-based algorithm to estimate variance components attributable to stratification and true additive genetic effects simultaneously, using data from the FarmGTEx project and large human biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) for validation. Sensitivity will be tested by simulating traits with known heritability under realistic stratification models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, open-source tool for the community to obtain stratification-corrected heritability estimates, directly addressing a key methodological gap. It will enhance the interpretability of heritability studies in structured populations and improve the resolution of efforts to map the genetic architecture of complex traits by reducing confounding bias.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard genomic control, which applies a single correction factor, our model directly estimates and removes the structured covariance induced by population stratification within the variance component estimation framework itself, offering a more nuanced and statistically principled correction.

proposals.title: Estimating Genetic Variance Components via Regularized Regression: A Machine Learning Framework for High-Dimensional SNP Data
proposals.motivation: Standard linear mixed models (LMMs) for variance component estimation, while foundational, rely on assumptions of additive effects and homogeneous variance contributions across SNPs. This can be limiting for capturing complex genetic architectures, including non-additive effects and the heterogeneous contributions of rare and common variants. A more flexible, data-driven approach is needed to improve the accuracy and interpretability of heritability estimates, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' problem highlighted in pre-2026 research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying machine learning regularization techniques to model SNP effects directly will provide more accurate and robust estimates of additive and non-additive genetic variance components than standard LMMs, particularly for traits with complex genetic architectures.
proposals.method: We will frame variance component estimation as a high-dimensional regression problem, where the total genetic value is a function of all SNP genotypes. Instead of using a single genetic relationship matrix, we will employ regularization methods like LASSO, elastic net, and group LASSO to estimate SNP effects while simultaneously partitioning variance. Specific SNP groupings (e.g., by allele frequency, functional annotation from resources like FarmGTEx) will be used in group regularization to estimate variance attributable to different variant classes. The method will be validated using simulated data and applied to real human (e.g., UK Biobank) and livestock (FarmGTEx) phenotypes, such as height.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified statistical framework for variance component estimation that moves beyond the limitations of the standard LMM. It will yield new software tools for the community and generate more precise estimates of how different genomic regions and variant classes contribute to heritability, offering deeper biological insight.
proposals.novelty: While regularization is used for SNP prediction, its systematic application for partitioning genetic variance into interpretable components—bridging machine learning and quantitative genetics parameter estimation—is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of pre-2026 methodologies.

proposals.title: Resolving the Missing Heritability of Complex Traits by Integrating Whole-Genome Sequence Data into Variance Component Models
proposals.motivation: A significant portion of the heritability of complex traits, known as 'missing heritability,' remains unexplained by common variants genotyped on SNP arrays. Whole-genome sequencing (WGS) data, now increasingly available in biobanks like UK Biobank, provides access to millions of rare and low-frequency variants hypothesized to contribute substantially to this gap. However, existing variance component models for heritability estimation, which rely on genomic relationship matrices (GRMs), are computationally intractable at the scale of tens of millions of WGS variants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing scalable algorithms to construct and utilize whole-genome sequence-based GRMs, we can capture the contribution of rare variants (MAF < 1%) and significantly increase the estimated narrow-sense heritability for a range of complex human phenotypes, thereby resolving a major portion of the missing heritability.
proposals.method: We will extend the GREML (Genomic-Relatedness-Based Restricted Maximum Likelihood) framework to WGS data. To manage computational burden, we will implement a block-wise, memory-efficient algorithm for constructing GRMs from millions of variants, potentially leveraging sparse matrix representations or frequency-dependent weighting schemes (e.g., MAF-stratified GRMs). We will apply this method to high-coverage WGS data from cohorts like UK Biobank, analyzing quantitative traits such as height and biomarkers, and partition heritability by minor allele frequency bins and functional annotations derived from resources like FarmGTEx to identify variant classes driving the heritability signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, large-scale estimates of the total additive genetic variance captured by all classes of variants in the human genome. It will quantify the specific contribution of rare variants to complex trait architecture and deliver a publicly available, scalable software tool for WGS-based heritability estimation, enabling the field to move beyond array data.
proposals.novelty: While variance component models are standard for array data, their direct application to full WGS data is computationally prohibitive. Our proposal's novelty lies in developing the specific algorithmic innovations required to make this application feasible, moving from theory to practice for the complete variant spectrum.

proposals.title: Inferring the Genetic Architecture of Complex Traits: A Bayesian Framework to Estimate Causal Variant Spectra from Heritability Estimates
proposals.motivation: A significant fraction of heritability for complex traits remains statistically 'missing' from genome-wide association studies, often attributed to rare variants, structural variants, or non-additive effects. Current methods estimate heritability from genetic data, but the inverse problem—inferring the underlying distribution of causal variant effect sizes and allele frequencies from a heritability estimate—is unsolved. Solving this is crucial for designing effective gene discovery studies and understanding evolutionary constraints on trait architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a heritability estimate, combined with population genetic priors, contains sufficient information to constrain the joint distribution of causal variant effect sizes and allele frequencies, allowing us to distinguish between architectures dominated by common, small-effect variants and those involving rare, larger-effect variants.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian inference framework. The model will parameterize the joint distribution of effect sizes (β) and allele frequencies (p) using flexible families (e.g., scale mixtures of normals for β, a Beta distribution for p). We will use a likelihood based on the expected relationship between the genetic variance contributed by a variant and its GWAS detection probability. Inference will be performed via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, integrating over the unobserved causal variants to fit the aggregate heritability estimate and other summary statistics (like the proportion of heritability explained by GWAS SNPs).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel tool to move from a single heritability estimate to a probabilistic description of the underlying genetic architecture. It will directly inform the design of future sequencing studies by predicting the required sample size to capture the missing heritability. The method will be applied to traits from the FarmGTEx project and human biobanks to produce concrete architectural predictions.
proposals.novelty: While previous work (e.g., Goddard et al. on gametic phase disequilibrium) has focused on explaining missing heritability, this proposal inverts the problem, using heritability as an input to infer architecture. It moves beyond simply partitioning heritability to actively reconstruct the properties of the unobserved causal variants.

proposals.title: Systematic Bias in Pedigree Heritability Estimates: Quantifying the Contribution of Epistasis, Rare Variants, and Non-Additive Effects to the 'Missing Heritability' Gap
proposals.motivation: The 'missing heritability' problem, defined as the gap between pedigree-based heritability (h²_ped) and SNP-based heritability (h²_snp), is a central challenge in complex trait genetics. This gap is often attributed to unmeasured rare variants or imperfect LD between SNPs and causal variants. However, an alternative, under-explored explanation is that h²_ped itself may be inflated for some traits due to non-additive genetic effects (e.g., epistasis, dominance) that contribute to phenotypic similarity among relatives but are not captured by additive SNP models. This potential inflation directly impacts the calculation and interpretation of missing heritability.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific complex traits, a significant portion of the reported pedigree heritability is attributable to non-additive genetic variance and the effects of rare variants segregating in families, leading to a systematic overestimation of the additive heritability 'missing' from GWAS.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale human (e.g., UK Biobank) and agricultural (FarmGTEx consortium) datasets with deep pedigree, whole-genome sequence, and phenotypic data. Using variance component models, we will partition the total pedigree-based variance into components attributable to: 1) common SNPs (additive GREML), 2) rare variants (using sequence-based kinship), and 3) non-additive effects (epistatic and dominance kernels). We will specifically test for traits where the sum of additive SNP and rare variant heritability approaches the pedigree estimate, indicating no missing heritability, versus traits where a significant non-additive pedigree component remains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative framework to dissect the sources of pedigree heritability, moving beyond the assumption that it represents a pure additive gold standard. It will recalibrate expectations for 'missing heritability' by identifying traits for which the gap is largely methodological (due to non-additivity) versus biological (truly unmeasured additive variants).
proposals.novelty: While non-additivity is known theoretically, this study directly quantifies its contribution to the pedigree-SNP heritability gap at scale, challenging the foundational assumption that 'missing heritability' is solely an additive signal awaiting discovery.

proposals.title: Comparative Partitioning of Complex Trait Heritability by Ancestry and Allele Frequency Using Whole-Genome Sequencing in Diverse Biobanks
proposals.motivation: Current estimates of SNP-based heritability and the contribution of rare variants are overwhelmingly derived from European-ancestry cohorts, limiting the generalizability of genetic architecture models. Major biobanks like the UK Biobank, All of Us, and Biobank Japan now offer large-scale whole-genome sequencing (WGS) data across diverse ancestries, providing an unprecedented opportunity to test the universality of these models. Understanding ancestry-specific differences in additive variance partitioning is critical for equitable genomic medicine and for resolving the biological basis of missing heritability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the proportion of additive genetic variance attributable to rare variants and the degree of gametic phase disequilibrium (GPD) bias in heritability estimates will vary systematically across global ancestries due to differences in demographic history, allele frequency spectra, and linkage disequilibrium structure.
proposals.method: We will apply the GREML-LDMS (Genomic-Relatedness-Based Restricted Maximum Likelihood – Linkage Disequilibrium and Minor Allele Frequency Stratification) framework, as used in Goddard's prior work on height, to WGS data from multiple ancestries within large biobanks. Genetic variants will be partitioned by minor allele frequency and local LD patterns within each ancestry group. Additive genetic variance components for complex traits (e.g., height, biomarkers) will be estimated for each stratum using GCTA or similar software, with careful adjustment for population structure. The analysis will explicitly quantify the GPD contribution to heritability estimates across these strata and ancestries.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, ancestry-stratified atlas of WGS-based heritability partitioning for major complex traits, directly quantifying the portability of European-centric models. It will identify traits and variant classes where genetic architecture is conserved or divergent across ancestries, informing the design of inclusive genomic studies. The results will provide empirical bounds for GPD bias in diverse populations, refining methods for cross-ancestry heritability estimation.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have estimated heritability in single non-European cohorts, a systematic, stratified variance component analysis using uniform WGS data and methods across multiple ancestries to dissect the roles of rare variants and GPD is novel. This moves beyond simple trans-ancestry genetic correlation to a detailed architectural comparison.

proposals.title: A Comprehensive Partitioning of Missing Heritability Across the Phenotypic Spectrum: From Common Diseases to Quantitative Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully mapped thousands of variants for many traits, yet a significant fraction of heritability remains statistically 'missing'. The framework developed by Goddard and colleagues to estimate and map this missing component has been applied to a foundational set of 34 traits, but its generalizability across the full spectrum of human complex phenotypes—from common diseases to quantitative physiological and molecular biomarkers—remains untested. A systematic application is needed to understand if the sources of missing heritability (e.g., rare variants, non-additive effects, gametic phase disequilibrium) are consistent or vary fundamentally across different phenotypic classes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative contributions of rare variants, non-additive genetic variance, and gametic phase disequilibrium to missing heritability are not uniform but systematically differ between broad categories of phenotypes, such as disease diagnoses, anthropometric traits, and blood biomarkers.
proposals.method: We will apply the established variance partitioning framework, which uses mixed models and LD score regression, to a curated set of >200 complex phenotypes from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank). The analysis will explicitly partition additive, non-additive, and rare variant variance components using whole-genome sequence and dense SNP array data. We will employ stratified heritability estimation across minor allele frequency (MAF) and linkage disequilibrium (LD) bins, and utilize the FarmGTEx resource as a comparative model to infer tissue-specific architectures. Statistical significance of differences between phenotypic categories will be assessed via block bootstrap procedures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale atlas of missing heritability sources, revealing which phenotypic domains are most amenable to discovery by emerging sequencing studies versus those requiring new models for non-additive variation. It will provide practical guidance for the design and powering of future genetic studies and refine biological models of complex trait architecture.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies estimated missing heritability for individual traits, this is the first systematic, cross-category investigation to test whether its underlying genetic causes are trait-type specific, moving from a trait-by-trait observation to a generalizable rule about genetic architecture.

proposals.title: Coalescent-based models of rare variant burden distribution for partitioning complex trait heritability
proposals.motivation: A significant portion of complex trait heritability, particularly for traits like height, is hypothesized to reside in rare variants not captured by standard GWAS. Current variance component models often treat rare variant load as a random effect with simplistic distributions, ignoring the deep population genetic processes that shape its variation across individuals. A principled, model-based distribution for rare variant burden is needed to improve heritability estimation and understand the genetic architecture of complex traits.
proposals.hypothesis: The distribution of an individual's total rare variant burden for a given trait can be accurately modeled by integrating coalescent theory with realistic demographic histories, and this modeled distribution will improve the precision of rare variant heritability estimates compared to standard assumptions.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation framework using msprime to generate genome sequences under published human demographic models (e.g., Out-of-Africa). We will assign effect sizes to rare variants (MAF<0.01) under different genetic architectures (e.g., negative selection). For each simulated individual, we will calculate a burden score. We will then fit parametric distributions (e.g., compound Poisson, gamma) to the empirical burden distribution and derive moment equations linking coalescent parameters to distribution parameters. Finally, we will implement a new variance component model in software like GCTA, replacing the standard random effect assumption with our derived distribution to re-estimate rare variant heritability for traits like height in the UK Biobank.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a population-genetically grounded statistical model for rare variant burden, moving beyond ad-hoc assumptions. It will deliver a novel method for partitioning heritability that more accurately attributes variance to rare variants, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' problem. The models and software will be a resource for the FarmGTEx and broader complex trait genetics community.
proposals.novelty: While coalescent theory is standard for modeling allele frequencies, its direct application to model the *distribution of total burden scores across individuals* for complex trait analysis is novel. This bridges a fundamental gap between theoretical population genetics and quantitative genetics estimation practice.
