proposals.title: Resolving Missing Heritability by Integrating Whole-Genome Sequence Variance Partitioning with Tissue-Specific Transcriptomic and Epigenomic Annotations
proposals.motivation: Despite the power of whole-genome sequencing (WGS) to capture rare variants, a substantial fraction of heritability for complex traits remains statistically 'missing' and biologically uninterpretable. Pre-2026 studies, including the FarmGTEx project, have generated rich multi-omics datasets, but these are rarely integrated with variance component models to directly quantify the contribution of different functional genomic layers to trait heritability. This gap limits our understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying genetic estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of WGS-based additive genetic variance, particularly from rare variants, can be statistically attributed to and explained by cis-regulatory variation captured in tissue-specific transcriptomic (eQTLs) and epigenomic (chromatin accessibility, histone marks) data from the same individuals.
proposals.method: We will extend GREML-like variance component models to partition WGS-based heritability estimates for traits like human height or bovine production metrics. Using individual-level WGS, RNA-seq, and ATAC-seq/ChIP-seq data from cohorts like FarmGTEx, we will construct multiple genetic relationship matrices (GRMs): one from all WGS variants, and others from variants subsetted based on their overlap with tissue-specific expression quantitative trait loci (eQTLs), open chromatin regions, or specific histone modifications. We will fit these GRMs jointly in a multi-component model using restricted maximum likelihood (REML) in software like GCTA, quantifying the variance attributable to each functional annotation class.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative map of how different regulatory genomic layers contribute to additive genetic variance, moving beyond simple SNP-trait associations. It will directly test if specific omics layers 'capture' missing heritability, offering a mechanistic bridge between estimated heritability and molecular function. The results will prioritize tissue and annotation contexts for future functional studies.
proposals.novelty: While variance partitioning using functional annotations from public databases exists, the integration of person-specific multi-omics data from the same cohort into a heritability estimation framework is novel. This approach directly tests if the regulatory architecture observed in an individual's omics profile informs their contribution to population-level genetic variance.

proposals.title: Rare Variant Polygenic Scores: A Novel Framework for Predicting Complex Disease Risk and Partitioning Missing Heritability
proposals.motivation: A significant portion of the heritability for complex traits and diseases remains unexplained by common variants identified in GWAS, a problem known as 'missing heritability.' Rare variants, which are poorly tagged by standard genotyping arrays and have low minor allele frequencies, are a leading candidate source for this missing component. Developing methods to aggregate their effects into predictive scores is crucial for improving genetic risk prediction and understanding the full genetic architecture of disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that aggregating the effects of rare, putatively functional variants across the genome into a polygenic score will significantly improve the prediction of individual disease risk for specific complex phenotypes, beyond the predictive power of common-variant polygenic scores alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a rare variant polygenic score (RV-PGS) framework using whole-genome sequencing data from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank). Rare variants (MAF < 0.01) will be aggregated into gene-based or functional unit scores using burden and variance-component tests (e.g., SKAT-O). These unit scores will then be weighted and combined into an individual-level RV-PGS using a penalized regression approach (e.g., LASSO or elastic net) trained on disease status, explicitly controlling for common-variant PGS. Predictive performance will be assessed via cross-validation and in independent cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable method for constructing polygenic scores from rare variants, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' problem. It will quantify the independent predictive contribution of rare variants to disease risk, potentially enabling more accurate individual risk stratification for clinical applications and refining our models of genetic architecture.
proposals.novelty: While rare variant association tests for groups of variants exist, the systematic construction of an individual-level, genome-wide polygenic score specifically from rare variants, and the direct comparison of its predictive power against and in combination with common-variant PGS, represents a novel and non-obvious integration of two major pre-2026 research strands.

proposals.title: Inferring Historical Selection Pressures from the Distribution of Rare Variant Heritability Across Complex Traits
proposals.motivation: A major challenge in evolutionary genetics is inferring the historical selection pressures that have shaped complex traits. While methods exist for detecting selection from allele frequency spectra, they are rarely applied to polygenic architectures. The recent availability of rare variant heritability estimates across many phenotypes provides a novel, aggregate signal of how genetic variation has been filtered by selection, offering a powerful lens into trait evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that traits under stronger historical purifying or stabilizing selection will exhibit a lower proportion of heritability explained by rare, functional variants compared to traits under weaker or diversifying selection, after accounting for differences in genetic architecture and ascertainment.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank) and rare variant heritability estimates from methods like GREML-LDMS or BOLT-REML. For a diverse set of complex traits, we will calculate the ratio of rare variant heritability (MAF < 0.01) to common variant heritability. We will then develop a hierarchical Bayesian model to infer a latent 'selection intensity' parameter for each trait from this ratio, using known correlates of selection (e.g., mutation load, association with fitness, evolutionary conservation) as prior information and controlling for confounding factors like SNP heritability and polygenicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic ranking of complex human traits by inferred historical selection strength, providing a quantitative framework to test evolutionary hypotheses. It will directly link contemporary genetic association data to evolutionary theory, offering a novel method to interpret the 'missing heritability' landscape and the evolutionary forces that shaped it.
proposals.novelty: While heritability estimation is mature, using the *distribution* of heritability across allele frequencies as an inverse problem to infer selection is a novel, aggregate approach that moves beyond single-variant selection scans and directly addresses the polygenic nature of complex traits.

proposals.title: Partitioning the Missing Heritability: An IBD-Based Variance Component Model for Additive and Non-Additive Genetic Effects
proposals.motivation: A significant portion of trait heritability, particularly for complex traits, remains unaccounted for by additive effects of common SNPs. Non-additive genetic variance, including dominance and epistasis, is a plausible contributor to this 'missing heritability' but is challenging to estimate accurately in human populations. Existing methods often rely on genomic relationship matrices (GRMs) from SNP data, which can be confounded by linkage disequilibrium and fail to capture long-range IBD sharing essential for modeling rare variant interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: Extending an Identity-By-Descent (IBD)-based variance component model to include explicit non-additive components will yield statistically robust estimates of dominance and additive-by-additive epistatic variance, thereby accounting for a significant portion of the heritability currently missing from additive SNP-based models.
proposals.method: We will extend the IBD-based framework, as used in Goddard's prior work on gametic phase disequilibrium, to model multiple variance components. Using dense SNP array and whole-genome sequence data (e.g., from UK Biobank), we will infer genome-wide IBD segments among individuals. We will then construct separate IBD-based relationship matrices for additive (A), dominance (D), and additive-by-additive epistatic (AxA) effects. These matrices will be incorporated into a mixed linear model using restricted maximum likelihood (REML) for variance component estimation, applied to quantitative traits like human height where non-additive effects are suspected.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, population-based estimate of the contribution of non-additive genetic variance to complex trait architecture, moving beyond theoretical bounds or simulation-based inferences. It will deliver a practical, IBD-based software tool for partitioning genetic variance, offering insights into the biological basis of missing heritability and informing models for genetic prediction.
proposals.novelty: While GRM-based models for non-additive variance exist, this approach uniquely leverages inferred IBD segments, which provide a more direct and accurate measure of shared genetic descent, potentially offering greater power to detect rare variant interactions and disentangle non-additive effects from additive variance inflation due to population structure.

proposals.title: Comprehensive Partitioning of SNP and IBD Heritability Across the Phenotypic Spectrum of the UK Biobank
proposals.motivation: Genomic-Relatedness-Based Restricted Maximum Likelihood using Identity-by-Descent segments (GREML-IBD) provides a powerful framework to estimate heritability explained by all genotyped SNPs and by rare variants not captured by arrays, via long IBD segments. While demonstrated on height, its application to a broad range of complex traits is limited. A systematic analysis across hundreds of UK Biobank phenotypes is needed to understand the general patterns of missing heritability, the contribution of rare variants, and the role of gametic phase disequilibrium for diverse trait architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the proportion of heritability captured by IBD segments relative to SNP-based methods will vary systematically across trait categories, being higher for traits with stronger evolutionary constraints and a significant burden of rare, penetrant variants.
proposals.method: We will apply GREML-IBD to ~500 quantitative and binary traits in the UK Biobank (N~500K). Genetic relatedness matrices (GRMs) will be constructed: one from all genotyped SNPs (SNP-GRM) and one from genome-wide IBD segments (IBD-GRM) inferred using refined methods like RaPID or hap-IBD. We will use GCTA's GREML analysis in a bivariate model to estimate variance components attributable to each GRM. Analyses will be stratified by trait domains (e.g., anthropometric, biochemical, disease, cognitive) and correlated with known genetic architecture metrics (e.g., SNP-heritability, polygenicity).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale atlas of IBD-based heritability estimates, directly quantifying the 'missing' heritability for many traits. It will empirically test the theoretical contribution of gametic phase disequilibrium across diverse phenotypes and inform the optimal design of future rare-variant association studies by identifying traits where IBD mapping is most fruitful.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond proof-of-concept on height, this study leverages the unique scale and phenotyping depth of the UK Biobank to perform a comparative population genetics analysis of SNP-based versus IBD-based heritability for virtually all major complex trait domains, a analysis not yet undertaken pre-2026.

proposals.title: Systematic Quantification of Environmental Confounding in GREML-IBD Heritability Estimates Across Trait Categories
proposals.motivation: Genomic-relatedness-based restricted maximum likelihood (GREML) using identity-by-descent (IBD) segments is a key method for partitioning phenotypic variance and estimating 'missing heritability' from rare variants. However, shared environment among relatives can inflate these estimates, a problem acknowledged but rarely quantified empirically across diverse traits. Given the increasing use of GREML-IBD in large biobanks and agricultural populations like those in FarmGTEx, understanding the magnitude and pattern of this confounding is critical for accurate interpretation of genetic architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degree of environmental confounding in GREML-IBD estimates varies systematically by trait category, being most severe for behavioral, educational, and social outcomes, and minimal for classical anthropometric traits like height.
proposals.method: We will analyze large, genetically informative cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, FarmGTEx partners) with known pedigree structures. For a range of phenotypes, we will compute GREML-IBD estimates using genome-wide SNP data and compare them to estimates derived from the known pedigree relationship matrix (expected to better capture shared environment). The difference (GREML-IBD minus pedigree-based estimate) will serve as our quantitative measure of environmental confounding. We will implement this using GCTA software, stratifying analyses by trait categories (anthropometric, physiological, behavioral, disease) and partitioning the genome by minor allele frequency to assess variant-specific confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical benchmark for the magnitude of environmental inflation in GREML-IBD estimates across trait domains. It will yield practical guidance for researchers on which trait categories require extreme caution in interpretation. The results will directly inform efforts to map missing heritability by clarifying the portion of variance attributable to rare variants versus familial environment.
proposals.novelty: While the potential for confounding is known, a systematic, trait-category-specific assessment using real-world data from large cohorts with both genomic and pedigree information has not been conducted. This moves beyond simulation studies to provide actionable, empirical evidence.

proposals.title: A Statistical Mechanics Framework for Perturbative Estimation of Epistatic Variance in Complex Traits
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of additive models in genome-wide association studies, a significant portion of trait heritability remains statistically 'missing'. A leading hypothesis is that non-additive genetic variance, particularly from epistatic interactions, contributes substantially but is poorly captured by standard linear models. Quantifying this contribution is crucial for understanding genetic architecture and improving predictive models.
proposals.hypothesis: The residual heritability of complex traits, after accounting for additive effects, can be explained in part by a network of low-order, widespread epistatic interactions, analogous to weak interactions in a many-body physical system.
proposals.method: We will adapt the cluster expansion method from statistical mechanics, formulating a Hamiltonian-like model where the phenotype is a function of single-variant 'fields' and pairwise/triplet interaction 'energies'. Using large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank), we will implement a perturbative inference framework, likely using a combination of regularized regression (e.g., LASSO) on interaction terms and variance component estimation via Haseman-Elston regression, to estimate the total variance attributable to these interactions. Simulations based on real genotype data will validate power and calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative estimate of the contribution of epistasis to heritability for key traits like height, moving beyond indirect evidence. It will deliver a novel, interpretable statistical framework for modeling genetic interactions that bridges concepts from quantitative genetics and physics.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly translating the formal perturbative tools of statistical mechanics (cluster expansion) to the problem of genetic variance partitioning, offering a principled alternative to brute-force testing of all interaction terms or purely theoretical models.

proposals.title: Generalizing Heritability Estimation: A Unified Method for Diverse Relative Pairs and Admixed Populations
proposals.motivation: Accurate estimation of narrow-sense heritability (h²) is foundational for understanding complex trait architecture. Traditional sibling-pair designs, while robust to population stratification, are restrictive and inefficient for many study cohorts, such as those with extended pedigrees, distant relatives, or admixed individuals. A method that can leverage diverse genetic relationships without bias from population structure would unlock heritability analysis for a wider array of valuable datasets, including biobanks and agricultural populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a likelihood-based heritability estimator, explicitly modeling the expected genetic covariance for any pair of individuals based on their genome-wide allele sharing and local ancestry, can provide unbiased estimates across diverse relative types and in admixed populations.
proposals.method: We will extend the linear mixed model framework, using the genomic relationship matrix (GRM) calculated from common variants. For general relative pairs, we will derive and implement the exact expected genetic covariance as a function of the genome-wide kinship coefficient. For admixed populations, we will integrate a local ancestry-aware GRM, where the covariance for each segment is weighted by the probability of shared ancestry, adapting methods from admixture mapping. Estimation will be performed via restricted maximum likelihood (REML) using optimized algorithms (e.g., AIREML or HE regression) to handle the modified covariance structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source tool for heritability estimation in underutilized study designs, increasing statistical power and resource efficiency. It will directly address the 'missing heritability' problem in underrepresented and admixed groups by enabling robust estimation in these populations, bridging gaps highlighted by the FarmGTEx and related projects.
proposals.novelty: While methods exist for siblings or homogeneous populations, a unified estimator that jointly accounts for arbitrary relatedness and admixture-induced covariance is novel. This moves beyond simply applying standard GREML to non-sibling pairs, which can be biased, by formally incorporating the genetic expectations of these more complex relationships.

proposals.title: Mapping the Genomic Dark Matter: Inferring the Functional Architecture of Missing Heritability from Polygenic Signal Distortions
proposals.motivation: A significant fraction of heritability for complex traits remains unaccounted for by genome-wide association studies (GWAS), representing a major gap in our understanding of genetic architecture. This 'missing heritability' is analogous to dark matter in cosmology—its presence is inferred from its aggregate effects but its specific locations and properties are obscured. Characterizing where this residual polygenic signal is functionally concentrated (e.g., in specific regulatory contexts) is a critical pre-2026 challenge for moving from genetic association to biological understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: The statistical 'gravitational' influence of GWAS-unaccounted polygenic variation distorts observable genomic data structures in systematic ways, and these distortion patterns can be used to infer the functional genomic compartments (e.g., cell-type-specific regulatory elements) where the missing heritability is most active.
proposals.method: We will adapt principles from weak gravitational lensing mass mapping. Using summary statistics from large-scale GWAS (e.g., for height) and reference data like FarmGTEx and 1000 Genomes, we will treat genome-wide linkage disequilibrium (LD) and expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) correlation matrices as the 'fabric' being distorted. We will develop a statistical model to decompose the observed variance-covariance structure of association signals into a component explained by detected variants and a residual component. This residual will be mapped onto functional annotations using a constrained optimization framework to identify annotations where the unexplained signal is disproportionately concentrated, akin to reconstructing a mass map from shear fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for characterizing the functional architecture of missing heritability, moving beyond simply estimating its magnitude. It will generate testable hypotheses about which biological pathways and cellular contexts harbor the bulk of undiscovered polygenic effects, guiding future functional genomics studies.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual and methodological analogy to cosmological dark matter mapping, applying a lensing-inspired framework to genomic data to localize hidden signal. It differs from existing variance partitioning methods by focusing on patterns of statistical distortion in genomic data structures rather than just variance explained.

proposals.title: Disentangling Causal Pathways in Polygenic Adaptation: A Structural Equation Model to Partition Genome-Wide Inflation of Linkage Statistics
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide scans for selection in polygenic traits often show pervasive inflation of linkage statistics, complicating the identification of true causal loci. This inflation is hypothesized to stem from a mix of direct selection on a locus, indirect selection via genetically correlated traits, and confounding population structure, but current methods only correct for it rather than explain it. Distinguishing these sources is critical for understanding the genetic architecture and evolutionary history of complex traits in humans, livestock, and natural populations.
proposals.hypothesis: The genome-wide pattern of inflation in selection statistics (e.g., LOD scores, p-values) can be formally decomposed into components attributable to direct causal effects of a locus on the focal trait versus indirect effects mediated by selection on genetically correlated traits, using a causal inference framework. This allows the inference of which traits were direct targets of polygenic selection.
proposals.method: We will develop a structural equation model (SEM) that integrates Mendelian Randomization principles with established genetic covariance and LD score regression frameworks. The model will treat genome-wide summary statistics (e.g., from GWAS of the focal trait and correlated traits) as inputs, modeling the observed inflation as a function of latent causal paths. We will employ a likelihood-based approach, using genomic partitioning and cross-trait LD score regression to estimate parameters, and validate the model using simulations and empirical data from the FarmGTEx project and human biobanks where selection signals and trait genetic architectures are well-characterized.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal causal model to move beyond correction for inflation in selection scans, turning a statistical nuisance into an informative signal about evolutionary history. It will deliver a method to map the causal pathways of polygenic adaptation, distinguishing direct selection from correlated response. This directly addresses the challenge of mapping 'missing heritability' of selection, analogous to the challenge of mapping missing heritability of trait variance.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods correct for genome-wide inflation or test for selection on individual loci, no pre-2026 framework uses the *pattern* of inflation across the genome to infer the causal network of traits under selection. The novelty lies in fusing causal inference (Mendelian Randomization/SEM) with population genetic models of polygenic selection to decompose the inflation signal.

proposals.title: Inverting the Genetic Signal: A Waveform-Inspired Model to Partition Heritability Across the Allele Frequency Spectrum
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in genome-wide association studies, a significant portion of trait heritability remains unexplained, particularly for rare variants. Standard SNP-based heritability estimators struggle to reliably partition this signal across the allele frequency spectrum, especially for very rare variants (MAF < 0.1%), due to limited statistical power and linkage disequilibrium complexities. A robust, model-based method to decompose heritability by allele frequency is crucial for understanding the genetic architecture of complex traits and the potential contribution of rare variation.
proposals.hypothesis: The statistical framework of seismic waveform inversion can be adapted to genetic data, allowing the total heritability estimated from family-based linkage disequilibrium (LD) methods to be decomposed into contributions from pre-defined allele frequency bins. This inversion will provide stable and interpretable estimates of rare variant heritability that are less susceptible to the biases of direct variance component estimation in sequencing studies.
proposals.method: We will adapt the theory of Full Waveform Inversion (FWI) to genetics. The core 'forward model' will be a linkage-informed heritability estimator (e.g., an extension of LD Score Regression or a Haseman-Elston regression using genomic relationships). In this model, variants stratified by MAF bins (e.g., ultra-rare, rare, low-frequency, common) act as subsurface layers with distinct 'impedance' properties (heritability contributions). We will implement an iterative inversion algorithm (e.g., a gradient-based optimization) that minimizes the misfit between the observed genome-wide genetic covariance and the covariance predicted by the forward model, thereby solving for the heritability parameter for each frequency bin.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled statistical tool for partitioning heritability by allele frequency using whole-genome sequencing data. It will directly integrate the robust properties of family-based heritability estimation with rare variant data, offering more reliable estimates of the contribution of rare variants to complex traits. The resulting software will be a significant resource for the FarmGTEx and similar consortia analyzing sequencing data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual cross-disciplinary transfer of a geophysical inversion framework to quantitative genetics, creating a model-based decomposition method. This moves beyond standard additive variance component models by treating the heritability estimator itself as a physical system to be inverted, providing a new pathway to integrate rare variant data.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Colocalization Framework for Disentangling Shared Genetic Architecture from Pleiotropy at Complex Trait Loci
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) consistently identify loci associated with multiple complex traits, but distinguishing true biological pleiotropy (a single causal variant affecting multiple traits) from linkage disequilibrium (LD) between distinct trait-specific variants remains a major challenge. This ambiguity hinders the interpretation of genetic correlations and the identification of causal pathways, which is central to understanding the 'missing heritability' and biological mechanisms underlying trait covariation. Existing colocalization methods often rely on simplifying assumptions about LD and causal variant architecture that may not hold across diverse trait pairs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian colocalization model, adapted from astrophysical signal analysis, can robustly distinguish between a single shared causal variant and multiple distinct variants at a locus by formally integrating GWAS summary statistics, LD structure, and realistic priors on allelic architecture and effect sizes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Bayesian framework used in astrophysics to colocalize signals from different instruments, treating GWAS summary statistics for two traits as analogous to measurements from different spectral bands. The core model will compute posterior probabilities for two hypotheses: H1 (a single causal variant shared between traits) and H2 (two distinct causal variants). We will implement a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) scheme to sample over the space of possible causal variants, using LD matrices from reference panels (e.g., 1000 Genomes) and incorporating flexible, trait-specific priors for variant effect sizes and the probability of a variant being causal, informed by pre-2026 heritability models like LD Score regression. The method will be validated using simulations and applied to real GWAS data for traits like height and related metabolic phenotypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous tool to quantify evidence for shared causal variants, directly addressing a key question in post-GWAS biology. It will enhance the interpretation of genetic correlations from studies like FarmGTEx and improve the prioritization of variants for functional follow-up by distinguishing pleiotropic hubs from coincidental co-localization. The method will contribute to mapping the genetic basis of trait covariation and refining heritability estimates partitioned by shared causal mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and technical transfer of a mature Bayesian colocalization framework from astrophysics to genetics, moving beyond correlation-based colocalization by explicitly modeling the competing causal variant hypotheses under a unified probabilistic model that accounts for LD and heterogeneous trait architectures in a principled way.

proposals.title: Integrating Colocalization Analysis and Frequency-Dependent Selection Models to Map Regulatory Variants in Evolutionarily Dynamic Complex Traits
proposals.motivation: A significant proportion of complex trait heritability, especially for fitness-related traits, is hypothesized to be influenced by frequency-dependent selection (FDS), where an allele's fitness depends on its population frequency. However, the specific regulatory mechanisms through which FDS acts remain largely unmapped. Pre-2026, large-scale resources like GTEx and FarmGTEx provide expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) maps, but methods to formally link these to loci under FDS are lacking, hindering a mechanistic evolutionary understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: Genetic loci associated with complex traits under frequency-dependent selection will show significant colocalization with cis-eQTLs, implicating specific regulatory variants and target genes as the direct targets of selection.
proposals.method: We will first identify trait-associated loci under FDS by applying models of frequency-dependent fitness (e.g., from population genetic theory) to GWAS summary statistics for relevant traits (e.g., disease susceptibility, morphological traits). For these candidate loci, we will perform formal Bayesian colocalization analysis (using the COLOC R package) against cis-eQTL data from relevant tissues in FarmGTEx and human GTEx v8. We will integrate these results with variance component models to estimate the proportion of additive genetic variance attributable to colocalized regulatory variants and use fine-mapping (e.g., SuSiE) to prioritize causal variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map linking evolutionary signals of frequency-dependent selection to specific regulatory pathways. It will advance the field beyond estimating 'missing heritability' by explaining its potential evolutionary causes and regulatory architecture. The framework will be a tool for predicting how selection pressures on gene expression may shift with allele frequency changes.
proposals.novelty: While colocalization is standard in molecular QTL studies, its integration with formal models of frequency-dependent selection to dissect the genetic architecture of evolutionarily dynamic traits is novel. This moves from correlating traits and expression to testing a specific evolutionary mechanism driving their shared genetic basis.

proposals.title: Fine-Scale Reconciliation of GWAS Loci: Dissecting the Contribution of Rare and Common Variants to Local Heritability
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide heritability analyses often reveal a gap between SNP-based heritability estimates and the heritability explained by known GWAS loci, the 'missing heritability' problem. While reconciliation frameworks have been applied genome-wide to partition heritability by variant frequency and functional annotation, the specific architecture within individual, well-replicated GWAS loci remains poorly characterized. Understanding whether the signal at a given locus is driven by a few common variants, a constellation of rare variants, or a combination is crucial for biological interpretation and designing functional follow-up studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a heritability reconciliation framework at the scale of individual GWAS loci will reveal distinct and heterogeneous architectures, where loci are differentially enriched for rare versus common variant contributions, and that this fine-scale partitioning will resolve a significant portion of the apparent 'missing heritability' within these regions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the GREML-LDMS framework (Genomic-Relatedness-Based Restricted Maximum Likelihood with Linkage Disequilibrium and Minor Allele Frequency Stratification) to analyze specific genomic regions. For each locus, we will construct multiple regional genetic relationship matrices (GRMs) stratified by variant frequency (e.g., MAF < 0.01, 0.01-0.05, >0.05) and functional annotation using pre-2026 resources like FarmGTEx and ENCODE. Using restricted maximum likelihood (REML) within a multivariate model, we will estimate the variance component attributable to each stratum for complex traits such as human height and bovine production traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fine-scale map of heritability architecture across major GWAS loci, directly linking statistical signals to potential causal variant spectra. It will quantify the contribution of rare variants to established loci, informing the design of deep resequencing studies and variant prioritization. The results will advance the mechanistic understanding of complex trait genetics beyond aggregate genome-wide summaries.
proposals.novelty: While GREML-LDMS is established for genome-wide partitioning, its application to dissect heritability at the single-locus level is novel. This shifts the unit of analysis from the whole genome to specific functional regions, moving from population-level summaries to locus-specific biological inference.

proposals.title: A Diagnostic Framework for Robust Sibling-Based Heritability Estimation in Ultra-Large Biobanks
proposals.motivation: Sibling-based designs are crucial for estimating heritability free from population stratification and assortative mating, but their application in biobanks with millions of individuals remains computationally challenging and diagnostically shallow. Current methods prioritize efficiency, producing a single point estimate without rigorously leveraging the scale of the data to validate the underlying model assumptions, such as the absence of indirect genetic effects or non-random mating, which can lead to biased inferences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding a scalable sibling-based estimator within a comprehensive diagnostic framework will yield heritability estimates that are not only computationally feasible for ultra-large biobanks but also accompanied by quantifiable confidence metrics and bias corrections, substantially improving interpretability and robustness.
proposals.method: We will develop a scalable computational system using distributed computing (e.g., Apache Spark) to apply the Haseman-Elston regression or similar sibling-based estimator to millions of pairs. The core will be extended with a suite of diagnostic modules that perform sensitivity analyses by partitioning data by genomic segments (e.g., MAF bins, LD blocks) and familial structures (e.g., sibling type, parental availability). These modules will systematically test for violations of key assumptions, such as assortative mating (via spousal correlations) and indirect genetic effects (via differences between sibling types), generating bias indicators and corrected estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a practical tool for producing diagnostically validated, robust heritability estimates from biobanks like UK Biobank and All of Us. It will advance the field by moving beyond point estimates to deliver a standardized assessment of estimate reliability, directly addressing the 'missing heritability' problem by clarifying which components are estimable with high confidence.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the core estimator, but in the systematic, automated diagnostic pipeline that exploits biobank scale to perform internal validation and sensitivity analysis, transforming a simple estimate into a rigorously vetted statistical result with explicit confidence metrics.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Architecture of Missing Heritability: Inferring Causal Variant Number and Effect Sizes from Linkage Disequilibrium Score Regression Inflation
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for complex traits consistently identify variants that explain only a fraction of the estimated SNP-based heritability, leaving a substantial 'missing heritability' gap. The observed inflation of test statistics in linkage disequilibrium score regression (LDSC), attributable to polygenicity and confounding, provides a potential signal about the underlying genetic architecture of these missing effects. Quantifying this signal is critical for understanding the power and limitations of current GWAS and for designing future studies to capture the full genetic component.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed LDSC intercept inflation for a trait can be inverted to estimate the number and average effect sizes of causal variants not yet detected by GWAS, under explicit models of genetic architecture including rare and common variant contributions.
proposals.method: We will develop a statistical framework that treats the observed LDSC intercept as a function of the number of causal variants, their effect size distribution, and their linkage disequilibrium (LD) with genotyped SNPs. Using simulations based on real genomic structure from the FarmGTEx and 1000 Genomes projects, we will model architectures ranging from many small-effect common variants to a mixture including rare variants of larger effect. We will then apply an inverse modeling approach, using optimization and Bayesian inference, to fit these models to real GWAS summary statistics for traits like height, estimating the most likely parameters (N_causal, effect size variance) that explain the observed inflation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-based estimate of the number and effect size spectrum of variants underlying missing heritability, moving beyond descriptive partitioning. It will deliver practical guidance on the sample sizes and variant discovery strategies (e.g., sequencing for rare variants) required for future GWAS to achieve saturation for specific traits.
proposals.novelty: While LDSC is used to estimate heritability and confounding, its intercept has not been systematically inverted to explicitly quantify the unseen causal variant landscape. This approach directly links a standard GWAS diagnostic (inflation) to the core problem of missing heritability architecture.

proposals.title: Quantifying Selection and Demographic Influences on Wild-Type Haplotype Segments in Complex Trait Architecture
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and heritability analyses often treat the 'wild-type' or major allele haplotype background as a neutral reference. However, the pervasive 'missing heritability' and the contribution of rare variants suggest that the genetic architecture of complex traits is not fully captured by additive models of identified variants. If the common haplotype segments themselves are shaped by selection or demographic history, this could bias heritability estimates and obscure the true distribution of causal variants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that short, common 'wild-type' haplotype segments, which are typically treated as neutral, exhibit signatures of polygenic selection and are influenced by population demographic history, thereby contributing to the variance attributed to 'missing heritability'.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-coverage whole-genome sequence data from large human (e.g., UK Biobank) and livestock (FarmGTEx) cohorts. Using phased haplotype data, we will segment genomes into 'wild-type' blocks based on the most common major allele haplotypes. We will then apply a combination of methods: 1) Variance component models to partition trait heritability specifically onto these segments, 2) Population genetic statistics (e.g., iHS, nSL, Tajima's D) calculated per segment to detect selection signals, and 3) Simulations using inferred demographic histories to distinguish selective from demographic effects on segment frequency and length distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a fundamental assumption in quantitative genetics, providing a novel framework to quantify non-neutrality in the haplotype background. It will refine models of missing heritability by attributing variance to selected haplotype segments and improve the interpretation of GWAS and genomic prediction results in both human and agricultural genetics.
proposals.novelty: While selection on trait-associated alleles is studied, explicitly testing the neutrality of the ubiquitous 'wild-type' haplotype background—the genomic canvas upon which variants are called—is a novel and critical inversion of the standard analytical perspective.

proposals.title: Inferring the Strength of Selection from Time to Most Recent Common Ancestor: A Coalescent-Based Framework for Complex Traits
proposals.motivation: A significant portion of the genetic architecture of complex traits, particularly the 'missing heritability', is hypothesized to be influenced by rare variants and non-additive effects, which are shaped by natural selection. Current methods for detecting selection, like iHS or PBS, often rely on allele frequency spectra or population differentiation, but struggle to quantify the precise selection coefficient (s) acting on a specific haplotype. An inverse method that infers s from the Time to Most Recent Common Ancestor (TMRCA), which is directly altered by selection, would provide a powerful, direct estimate of selective pressure on trait-associated haplotypes.
proposals.hypothesis: The TMRCA distribution of a haplotype carrying a selected variant is a predictable function of the selection coefficient and demography, such that given an estimated TMRCA (e.g., from Relate or ARGweaver) and a known demographic model, we can accurately infer the strength of selection that shaped its history.
proposals.method: We will develop a coalescent-with-selection simulation framework using msms or SLiM to generate the joint distribution of TMRCA and allele frequency under a range of selection coefficients (s) and demographic models (e.g., inferred from FarmGTEx or 1000 Genomes data). We will then construct a likelihood function or train a machine learning model (e.g., a neural network) to perform the inverse mapping: from observed TMRCA and frequency to an estimated s. The method will be validated on simulated data and applied to haplotypes associated with height and other complex traits from large biobanks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel statistical tool to directly estimate selection coefficients on specific haplotypes, bridging population genetics and complex trait architecture. It will offer new insights into the evolutionary forces shaping 'missing heritability' and help distinguish between additive and non-additive (e.g., dominance, epistasis) selective models by comparing inferred s to estimates from additive variance partitioning.
proposals.novelty: While forward simulations and demographic models are standard, the explicit inversion of the TMRCA distribution to infer a precise selection coefficient for a specific haplotype, integrating with contemporary ARG inference and complex trait data, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of coalescent theory and quantitative genetics.

ideas.title: A Causal Model for Polygenic Selection: Disentangling Direct and Indirect Effects from Inflated Linkage Signals
ideas.description: This research develops a formal causal model that explains the genome-wide inflation of linkage statistics (like LOD scores or p-values) for polygenic traits as a mixture of direct causal effects and indirect correlative effects. By leveraging Mendelian randomization principles and structural equation modeling within a selection inference framework, the model can separate the portion of inflation attributable to direct selection on a locus from inflation caused by selection on genetically correlated traits or from confounding population structure. This allows researchers to move beyond simply correcting for inflation and instead use the pattern and magnitude of inflation across the genome to infer the causal pathways of polygenic adaptation, distinguishing which traits were direct targets of selection versus which changed as a correlated response.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Waveform Inversion to Partition Heritability by Allele Frequency
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic waveform inversion to the problem of partitioning heritability by allele frequency. In seismology, waveform inversion decomposes a complex signal to attribute it to specific subsurface layers. Analogously, we propose to treat the linkage-informed heritability estimator as the core physical model for genetic 'signal propagation'. By inverting this model against whole-genome sequencing data, where variants are pre-stratified by frequency (e.g., MAF < 0.001%, 0.001-0.1%, 0.1-1%, >1%), we can decompose the total heritability estimate. This provides a model-based partition of heritability across the allele frequency spectrum, moving beyond standard SNP-based methods to directly integrate rare variant data from sequencing studies with the robust properties of family-based heritability estimation.

ideas.title: Robust, Diagnostically-Validated Estimation in Ultra-Large Biobanks
ideas.description: This research develops a scalable computational framework for sibling-based analyses in biobanks that prioritizes robustness over mere efficiency. It implements the core estimator for millions of pairs but embeds it within a suite of diagnostic procedures that leverage the biobank's size to test for model assumptions like the absence of assortative mating or indirect genetic effects. The system automatically performs sensitivity analyses across genomic and familial subsets, providing confidence metrics and bias-corrected estimates. The final output is not just a population estimate, but a rigorously vetted and interpretable result that quantifies its own reliability.

ideas.title: Colocalization of Genetic Loci Under Frequency-Dependent Selection
ideas.description: This research direction applies advanced colocalization methods from eQTL studies to test whether genomic loci associated with complex traits under frequency-dependent selection share causal variants with loci regulating gene expression. By integrating formal colocalization tests with models of frequency-dependent fitness, it can distinguish loci where allele frequency changes are likely driven by selection on gene expression levels from those with other mechanisms. This allows for the evolutionary prediction system to pinpoint specific regulatory pathways through which frequency-dependent selection acts, offering a mechanistic understanding of how selection pressures shift with trait prevalence. The result is a powerful framework to map the genetic architecture of evolutionarily dynamic traits to their regulatory underpinnings.

ideas.title: Disentangling hypothesis from artifact: A predictive simulation-empirical framework for the rarity of observed stabilizing selection
ideas.description: This study first builds an evolutionary simulation that explicitly models both the proposed novel mechanism for rarity and the suite of established methodological reasons stabilizing selection is hard to detect. It uses this model to generate unique, quantitative signatures in multivariate data—such as specific patterns of temporal fitness surface curvature, trait-fitness covariance, and among-individual variance—that distinguish the novel hypothesis from measurement artifact. These a priori predictions are then rigorously tested against a curated meta-dataset of long-term individual-based studies with high-precision repeated phenotyping and fitness estimates, allowing for a direct causal assessment of the hypothesis's explanatory power relative to conventional critiques.

ideas.title: Nonlinear mapping of genetic architecture to diagnose and resolve linkage failures
ideas.description: This research direction applies machine learning models like neural networks and Gaussian Processes to learn the complex, nonlinear mappings between genetic variants and phenotypic traits specifically for phenotypes where classical linkage studies famously failed. The core synergy is that the learned mappings themselves become interpretable objects, revealing whether the failure stemmed from extreme polygenicity, epistatic networks, or context-dependent effects that linear models could not capture. This transforms the 'negative' result of linkage into a positive classification of the trait's architectural class, providing a principled reason for the failure and guiding the choice of appropriate future study designs. Ultimately, it inverts the problem, using methodological failure as a selective filter to discover and model traits with the most complex genetic architectures.

ideas.title: Adapting Quantum Decoherence Methods to Disentangle Non-Separable Genetic Effects
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that additive and epistatic genetic effects are separable in nonlinear systems. Drawing an analogy from quantum information theory, where the non-separability (entanglement) of particles is a core feature, we adapt its solution framework. In quantum mechanics, entanglement is detected and quantified using tools like entanglement witnesses, not by forcing a separable description. The adapted idea is to develop analogous 'non-separability witnesses' for complex traits—statistical or computational operators applied to genomic and phenotypic data that can signal and measure the degree to which additive and epistatic effects are fundamentally intertwined. This shifts the research goal from attempting to partition variance into independent components to directly characterizing their joint, non-separable architecture as a property of the nonlinear genotype-phenotype map.

ideas.title: Adapting Dark Matter Mapping to Characterize Residual Heritability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the cosmological solution for mapping dark matter via gravitational lensing to the problem of characterizing residual heritability in genetics. In cosmology, the invisible mass of dark matter is mapped by analyzing its gravitational effects on visible light. Analogously, the 'dark matter' of the genome—the polygenic variation unaccounted for by GWAS—can be characterized by analyzing its statistical 'gravitational' effects on observable genomic data. The adapted research plan is to develop and apply methods that treat GWAS-unaccounted heritability as an unseen mass exerting influence, using patterns of statistical distortion in genome-wide association signals (e.g., subtle, polygenic signatures in linkage disequilibrium structure or gene expression correlations) to infer the functional genomic 'locations'—such as specific cell types, chromatin states, or gene networks—where this missing heritability is concentrated, even if the specific causal variants remain undetected.

ideas.title: A Causal and Operational Test for Fundamental Non-Separability of Genetic Effects in Nonlinear Phenotypes
ideas.description: This research develops a formal, operational definition of fundamental non-separability grounded in the non-commutativity of genetic perturbations, meaning the phenotypic effect of altering gene A then B differs from altering B then A. It designs an experimental-computational pipeline to apply this test, using precise combinatorial mutagenesis in model systems to perform ordered genetic perturbations and measure their outcomes. Concurrently, it employs deep neural networks trained on population genomic data to predict perturbation outcomes and identify candidate non-commutative interactions in silico. The convergence of empirical and computational evidence provides a falsifiable criterion to distinguish true mechanistic inseparability from mere statistical non-additivity.

ideas.title: Causal Inference of Epistatic Interaction Networks from Observational Phenotypic Data
ideas.description: This research direction integrates causal discovery algorithms from epidemiology with models for partitioning genetic variance to infer the causal structure of epistatic networks from observational genomic and phenotypic data. It aims to distinguish which additive effects are primary drivers and which are modifiers within higher-order interactions, revealing the directed influence and potential developmental sequence of genetic interactions. The resulting models would provide a mechanistic, rather than merely statistical, map of how epistasis shapes complex traits, offering testable hypotheses about biological pathways and intervention points. This approach fundamentally shifts the goal from variance description to causal network discovery of the genetic interactome.

ideas.title: Adapt Bayesian colocalization from astrophysics for multi-trait GWAS locus sharing
ideas.description: This idea proposes a method transfer from astrophysics to statistical genetics. In astrophysics, Bayesian colocalization is used to determine if signals across different electromagnetic spectra (e.g., optical and X-ray) at the same sky coordinates originate from a single celestial object. This solution is adapted to the problem of testing linkage-GWAS locus sharing. The adapted research idea employs a Bayesian colocalization framework to formally test if genetic associations for two distinct complex traits at a shared genomic locus are driven by a single shared causal variant or multiple distinct variants. This involves modeling GWAS summary statistics under hypotheses of a single causal variant versus independent variants, rigorously accounting for linkage disequilibrium and differing trait architectures, analogous to how astrophysics accounts for measurement errors across different instruments.

ideas.title: Applying Statistical Mechanics of Interacting Particles to Model Non-Additive Genetic Variance
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that residual heritability is purely additive by drawing an analogy from statistical physics. In condensed matter systems, the properties of a whole (e.g., magnetization, pressure) cannot be predicted from the sum of non-interacting parts; interaction terms are crucial. Similarly, in genetics, the phenotypic variance may depend on networks of interacting genetic variants (epistasis), not just their additive sum. The analogous solution—using cluster expansion or simulation methods to quantify interaction energies—is adapted by proposing a statistical-genetic framework that explicitly models variant-variant interactions in a Hamiltonian-like model. This approach would use large biobank-scale data to perturbatively estimate the contribution of non-additive genetic interaction networks to heritability, providing a direct test and potential quantification of their role in the so-called 'missing heritability'.

ideas.title: Leveraging Distributed Computing and Data Federation for Large-Scale Sibling Cohort Analysis
ideas.description: This idea proposes to improve the precision of the recombination-stratified heritability estimator by applying it to much larger collections of sibling pairs. The core challenge is logistical: assembling a single, centralized dataset of millions of genotyped sibling pairs with phenotypes is often infeasible due to privacy regulations and data governance. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where scientists precisely measure cosmic signals from petabytes of data distributed across global observatories, we adapt the solution of federated data architectures and distributed computing. We will develop a secure, privacy-preserving framework that allows the heritability estimation algorithm to run in parallel on sibling data held in multiple, separate biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, All of Us, FinnGen). The framework will coordinate the analysis, sharing only necessary, anonymized summary statistics for meta-analysis, thereby creating a virtual mega-cohort. This approach adapts the technical solution from astrophysics to overcome the data aggregation bottleneck in human genetics, enabling an unprecedented scale of sibling-based analysis for refined heritability estimation.

ideas.title: Quantifying Inflation in Linkage Statistics via Cosmological Parameter Estimation Frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from observational cosmology—specifically, the modelling of foreground contamination in the cosmic microwave background power spectrum—to the problem of genome-wide inflation in linkage statistics for polygenic traits. In cosmology, hierarchical Bayesian models are used to parameterize and separate inflationary foreground signals from the underlying true cosmological signal. Adapted to genetics, the idea is to develop a formal statistical model that treats the observed distribution of linkage statistics (e.g., LOD or Z scores) as a mixture. One component represents the true signal from multiple quantitative trait loci (following a specific polygenic architecture), while the other component explicitly models the systematic inflation due to factors like cryptic relatedness or population structure. By fitting this model to genome-wide data using likelihood or Bayesian estimation techniques, researchers can simultaneously quantify the parameters of the polygenic trait architecture and the magnitude and nature of the inflation mechanism, providing a principled correction and deeper insight into the genetic architecture.

ideas.title: Adapting Signal Detection Methods from Astrophysics to Isolate Major Loci in Polygenic Traits
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard polygenicity assumption in linkage analysis by adapting a signal detection solution from astrophysics. In exoplanet detection, phase-folding is used to isolate a weak, periodic signal from a noisy background. Analogously, in genetics, we can adapt this by treating the inheritance pattern of a chromosomal region containing a putative major locus as a 'periodic' signal within pedigree data. By computationally aligning (folding) family genotypes and phenotypes around a test position and stacking the linkage evidence across multiple families or large pedigrees, the method aims to enhance the detection signal of the major locus. This should allow the reconciliation framework (e.g., between linkage and association studies) to be tested specifically for traits where a major locus is embedded within a polygenic background, as the adapted technique seeks to isolate its contribution more cleanly.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Signal Processing to Negative Linkage Studies
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from geophysics to solve the problem of understanding traits with famously negative linkage results. In seismology, the failure to detect clear signals from deep structures was overcome by analyzing seismic noise and ambient wavefields using array processing techniques, revealing previously hidden subsurface features. Similarly, for genetically complex traits where linkage studies failed, we propose treating the genome-wide data not as failed linkage signals but as a genetic 'noise field' containing hidden architecture. By applying adapted signal processing methods—such as cross-correlation of allele sharing patterns across families and array-based detection of distributed polygenic effects—to the original family datasets, we can extract the subtle, polygenic architectural reasons why traditional LOD score approaches failed. This represents a dual scaling approach: scaling down the expectation from major loci to micro-effects, while scaling up the analytical method from single-point tests to genome-wide pattern recognition.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography for Fine-Scale Genomic Locus Architecture
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from seismic tomography in geophysics to the problem of dissecting individual GWAS loci. In seismology, waves from distributed events are used to image subsurface structures. Analogously, we propose applying the reconciliation framework at a fine scale by treating a genomic locus as a volume to be imaged. Diverse molecular phenotypic data (like ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, Hi-C) from many cell types or conditions serve as the 'wave sources' traversing the locus. The adapted solution is to develop a computational model that performs an integrative, inverse calculation on these diverse data layers. This will reconcile them into a unified, high-resolution map of the locus's regulatory architecture, pinpointing causal variants, enhancers, promoters, and their spatial interactions, thereby moving from association to mechanistic understanding.

ideas.title: Causal Network Discovery from Systematic Perturbations for Robust Developmental Modeling
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond assuming a known network model by actively learning the causal developmental network from data. It combines large-scale experimental perturbations (e.g., CRISPR-based gene knockouts, timed inhibitor treatments) with dense longitudinal multi-omics profiling to generate a causal inference dataset. Machine learning methods, such as constraint-based causal discovery and neural ordinary differential equations, are then applied to infer the direct regulatory relationships and their nonlinear dynamics without a pre-specified map. The resulting model is validated by its ability to predict the outcomes of novel combinatorial perturbations not used in training, ensuring it captures the fundamental system mechanics rather than fitting a preconceived structure.

ideas.title: Apply coalescent theory to model IBD sharing using a polymer physics framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from polymer physics, where the probability of spatial contact between chain segments is derived from statistical mechanical models of polymer conformation, to the problem of modeling Identity-by-Descent (IBD) sharing. In the analogous field, the contact probability between monomers decays predictably with their separation along the chain, influenced by physical constraints. By mapping chromosomal segments to polymers and coalescence events to 'contacts,' we can use a modified Gaussian chain or confined polymer model to theoretically frame IBD sharing. The adapted solution will derive the expected distribution of IBD segment lengths and frequencies across the genome, explicitly incorporating recombination as a distance metric along the chromosomal contour and population demographic parameters as factors influencing the 'chain's' statistical properties, thereby creating a novel coalescent-theory-based predictive framework for recombination-stratified IBD patterns.

ideas.title: Adapt Signal Decomposition Techniques from Audio Engineering to Partition Genetic Variances
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solution strategies from audio signal processing to the problem of separating additive from epistatic genetic variances. In audio, techniques like Fourier and wavelet transforms decompose a complex signal into linear, additive components (fundamental frequencies) and non-linear, interactive ones (harmonics, distortion). Similarly, we propose developing genetic variance models that treat phenotypic data as a 'signal' to be decomposed. By applying adapted spectral or sparse coding methods, we can create efficient, biologically motivated models that mathematically isolate the additive genetic variance (the linear fundamental) from the epistatic variance (the non-linear interactions), providing a new, computationally tractable framework for understanding genetic architecture.

ideas.title: Predict regional linkage statistics using a physics-inspired graph neural network on genomic feature networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational physics and materials science, where Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are used to predict macroscopic material properties from a network of atomic-scale features. The analogous problem is predicting a global property (linkage) from interconnected local features (genomic annotations). We adapt this by constructing a graph where nodes are genomic regions (e.g., LD blocks or fixed-size windows), with features derived from functional annotations, conservation, and regulatory data. Edges are defined by known long-range interactions (e.g., Hi-C data) or functional similarity. A GNN model is then trained to propagate information across this biological network, learning to integrate the complex, non-local dependencies between genomic features to predict regional linkage statistics (e.g., LOD scores) directly from GWAS summary statistics, offering a spatially-aware alternative to standard ML models.

ideas.title: Adapting Distributed Graph Processing Frameworks for Sibling-Pair Analysis
ideas.description: The challenge of scaling genetic analyses to millions of sibling pairs in biobanks is analogous to processing massive, sparse graphs in network science. The analogous solution employs distributed graph processing frameworks (e.g., Pregel/Giraph) that use a vertex-centric Bulk Synchronous Parallel (BSP) model to efficiently handle billions of connections. This can be adapted by modeling the biobank dataset as a graph where individuals are vertices and sibling relationships are edges. The core genetic analysis algorithm (e.g., for sibling-based heritability estimation) is reformulated into a series of supersteps. In each superstep, vertices (individuals) compute on local genetic data, send messages (e.g., simulated genotypes, intermediate statistics) along sibling edges, and then update their state based on received messages. This approach allows the problem to be partitioned across a distributed computing cluster, minimizing data shuffling and enabling efficient, iterative computation on ultra-large sibling networks derived from biobanks.

ideas.title: A Closed-World Experimental Platform for Validating Causal Inference in Selection
ideas.description: This research constructs a biomolecular implementation of a known causal system to serve as a validation benchmark for disentangling correlation from causation. It uses a synthetic microbial community in a chemostat with fully monitored and controlled environmental parameters (e.g., nutrient gradients, stressors) and continuous, high-resolution tracking of population dynamics and traits. By establishing ground-truth causal relationships through deliberate intervention, the system provides a empirical testbed to rigorously evaluate and refine causal inference methods, ensuring they can reliably identify direct selection effects before application to noisy, open-world observational data.

ideas.title: Adapt Geophysical Inverse Problem Solvers for Trait Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from geophysical inverse problems—where sparse, indirect measurements are mapped to complex subsurface structures—to the challenge of learning nonlinear trait mappings from high-dimensional biological data. In geophysics, physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and structured Gaussian processes embed known physical laws (e.g., wave equations) to regularize ill-posed mappings. Here, we propose using neural networks or GPs that embed biological constraints—such as pathway connectivity, regulatory interactions, or physiological scaling principles—as inductive biases in the model architecture or kernel. This approach learns mappings that are not only data-driven but also consistent with prior biological knowledge, improving robustness and interpretability when inferring trait relationships from genomic, transcriptomic, or phenomic datasets.

ideas.title: Apply causal discovery from climate science to infer developmental pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal discovery solutions from climate science, where scientists infer causal networks from complex, noisy, observational time-series data of the Earth system, to the domain of developmental science. In climate science, algorithms like PCMCI are used to disentangle lagged causal relationships and feedback loops between variables like atmospheric CO2, global temperature, and ice sheet extent from purely observational records. The adapted research idea applies these robust, nonlinear causal discovery methods to high-dimensional longitudinal datasets tracking human development (e.g., from birth through adolescence). By treating developmental factors (genetic, environmental, social, physiological) as nodes in a dynamic system, we can infer the causal structure of developmental pathways—identifying which early factors causally influence later outcomes, pinpointing critical intervention points, and uncovering bidirectional or feedback relationships, all from observational data.

ideas.title: Predict evolutionary trajectories using climate modeling ensemble techniques
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ensemble modeling approach from climate science to the problem of predicting medium-term evolutionary trajectories. In climate science, ensembles of models with varied parameters are used to project probable climate futures and their uncertainties over decades, rather than predicting a specific weather event. Similarly, we propose running an evolutionary framework (e.g., a population genetics simulator or digital life platform) as an ensemble, with systematic variation in key parameters like initial genetic diversity, mutation spectra, and environmental stochasticity. The synthesis of these runs generates a probabilistic prediction of evolutionary trajectories—highlighting likely phenotypic endpoints, potential evolutionary pathways, and critical branching points—while explicitly quantifying the uncertainty inherent in the prediction over multiple generations.

ideas.title: Apply signal detection theory to distinguish true stabilizing selection from measurement error
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the core solution from signal detection theory to the problem of rarely observed direct stabilizing selection. In signal processing, the difficulty of detecting a weak signal in noise is addressed by mathematically comparing the distributions of 'signal+noise' and 'noise alone' to define detectability (d'). Analogously, we propose to build a model where the 'signal' is the true stabilizing selection function and the 'noise' encompasses all confounding factors like genetic drift, environmental variance, and phenotypic measurement error. By applying this framework, the research would systematically quantify the probability that a given empirical study would fail to detect a genuine stabilizing selection gradient, thereby testing the hypothesis that its apparent rarity is often a consequence of low statistical power and high 'ecological noise' rather than its true absence.

ideas.title: Theory-Guided Detection of Regime Shifts in Developmental Nonlinearity
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply allowing relationships to change by embedding the search for change within a formal, hypothesis-driven framework. Candidate mechanisms (biological, contextual, or temporal) are explicitly stated as potential drivers for shifts in the form of a nonlinear relationship. The research system then employs principled statistical and machine learning techniques, heavily penalized for overfitting, to test if a change-point model aligned with the proposed mechanism is both necessary and sufficient to explain the data. This results in models that are more interpretable, generalizable, and directly tied to theoretical constructs about development and adaptation.

ideas.title: Model Developmental Noise as a Stochastic Differential Equation with a Developmental Potential
ideas.description: This idea challenges the deterministic view of nonlinear development by formally integrating random developmental noise using an analogy from statistical physics. The analogous problem—modeling the random motion of a particle subject to both deterministic and stochastic forces—was solved with the Langevin equation. Adapting this solution, we propose modeling developmental trajectories as paths governed by a Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE). The equation consists of a deterministic 'developmental potential' function that captures canalizing or attracting nonlinear dynamics, plus an additive or multiplicative noise term representing intrinsic stochasticity in molecular and cellular processes. This framework allows researchers to quantify the relative contributions of deterministic programming and stochastic noise to phenotypic outcomes, predict distributions of possible phenotypes from identical genotypes, and explore how noise can be harnessed or suppressed in evolutionary and biomedical contexts.

ideas.title: Adaptive Nonlinear Functions via Spline-Based Developmental Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of constant nonlinear relationships in development by allowing the functional form to change across environments or life stages. The analogy comes from geophysics, where the relationship between seismic velocity and depth changes form across different rock strata, solved using adaptive spline regression with data-determined knots. Adapted to developmental research, this proposes using flexible, piecewise regression models where the positions of transition points ('knots') between life stages or environmental contexts, as well as the polynomial order of the relationship within each segment, are inferred from the data. This creates an 'adaptive nonlinearity' where the model's fundamental form is not fixed a priori but can vary to best fit the local context, providing a more nuanced and accurate representation of development.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Parameterize Developmental Networks
ideas.description: The original idea proposes modeling nonlinear developmental relationships as dynamic biological networks. Drawing an analogy from Ecology, where Species Distribution Models (SDMs) parameterize a species' niche from environmental variables to predict its spatial distribution, we adapt this solution. Here, we treat a cell's developmental fate as a 'niche' within a multidimensional signaling landscape. By applying adapted SDM algorithms (e.g., MaxEnt) to single-cell genomic and proteomic data, we can data-drively parameterize the system, defining the attractor states, stability basins, and transition thresholds of the developmental network, thereby quantifying how nonlinear interactions govern cell fate decisions.
